tickets.summary: Orbis subset ETLs intermittently fail at validate stage due to shared metadata resolution
tickets.description:
Orbis CSV and Parquet subset ETLs intermittently fail during validate_raw_data with unresolved column errors. This appears to be caused by runs reading inconsistent or overwritten metadata during validation, rather than a pure schema issue in the source data.
We have a recurring failure pattern where Orbis subset ETLs (CSV and Parquet) fail in the validate raw stage with AnalysisException errors for missing / mismatched columns.
Typical errors include:
• Orbis parquet: unresolved column adv_original_advisor_function_in_english_

• Orbis CSV: unresolved column more_evaluation_year
These failures are consistent with the validate step picking up metadata that does not correspond to the data being validated (i.e. metadata produced by a different run or variant).
This issue has been observed multiple times across retries:
• Restarting from validate sometimes works

• Other times a full restart from import raw is required

• Ordering of runs appears to influence which variant succeeds
This strongly suggests a shared or non-isolated metadata resolution problem, rather than deterministic schema errors in the raw data itself.
We’ve tried introducing a distinct documentType for orbis_csv_subset to avoid metadata clashes. Despite this, the same validate-stage failures still occur, indicating the underlying issue is not fully addressed by config-only changes.
Suspected root cause (high level)
• Metadata used during validation is not reliably isolated per run / variant

• Runtime resolution may be:
• falling back to defaults (e.g. from reference.conf), or

• using values baked into Scala code, or

• resolving metadata paths inconsistently under concurrent or closely timed runs
This leads to CSV and Parquet subset runs reading incompatible metadata and failing validation.
Impact
• Nightly subset ETLs fail intermittently

• Manual intervention and reruns required

• Fragile behaviour when multiple Orbis variants are active

• Potentially affects other datasets sharing similar patterns
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

Investigate a small set of upcoming DevEx and AI tooling improvements emerging from Applications / Platform work to determine whether they should be adopted within DEAD repos.
This spike is exploratory only and aims to assess feasibility, value, dependencies, and rollout complexity — not to implement changes.
Items in Scope

2️⃣ Focus Mode CLI (Local Dev Performance)
• Understand how the Focus Mode CLI and Gradle plugin work.

• Evaluate potential benefits for DEAD repos (build speed, IDE usability).

• Identify risks, limitations, and suitability given our repo structures.
3️⃣ LLM-Based Build Triage / Analysis

• Understand how it detects causes (e.g. pre-emptible node loss) and proposes fixes.

• Assess whether this could help with DEAD build stability and DevOps load once proven in saas-banking.
Out of Scope

• No changes to production pipelines.

• No rollout commitments.

tickets.summary: [INCIDENT] OS Incremental Failure in Prod - Elastic Client Timeout
tickets.description:
The OpenSanctions incremental ETL in ochre-1-dead-prod-1 failed due to a recurring Elastic client timeout that occurs during the "orphaned records" cleanup step inside Fusion/QP code. This matches the same category of failures previously observed for OC and D&B incremental loads.
Performance Degradation:
The load_elastic step took approximately 6 hours 30 minutes instead of the normal 6 minutes runtime, representing a 6400% increase in execution time.
Airflow DAG Run: https://arbiter.quantexa.com/airflow2/dags/etl_opensanctions_incremental_main/grid?tab=graph&dag_run_id=scheduled__2025-12-09T08%3A00%3A00%2B00%3A00&task_id=load_elastic.spark_submit
Error seen in YARN logs:
Suppressed: java.lang.RuntimeException: Searching for orphaned records failed whilst looking for 1000 taking.
Caused by: java.net.SocketTimeoutException: 60,000 milliseconds timeout on connection http-outgoing-116 [ACTIVE]This occurs at the point where DeleteUtils.deleteResolverRecordsWithOldRunIdsAsync performs an Elastic read/delete scan. The network timeout is not treated as a transient error, so the ES client does not retry, causing the Spark task to fail after multiple executor attempts and abort the entire job.
This is a Fusion-owned area of the Elastic client, not part of DataOps or Data Feeds code. A similar issue was previously deep-dived by Tristan, with relevant code changes already logged here:
🔗 https://github.com/quantexa-internal/quantexa-platform/commit/38b47d2f2e9a824100cec6436e2759695122c97b
Impact:
• Prod incremental run experiencing severe performance degradation (6+ hours vs 6 minutes)

• Requires manual intervention and causes large delays in data availability

• Reoccurs across multiple datasources (OS, D&B, OC) under similar load conditions

• Retry logic is too strict → "connection reset" and similar network blips are not classified as transient, so no retry occurs

• Only affects ochre-1-dead-prod-1, not ochre-1-dead-dev-1, suggesting infrastructure-related issue
YARN Log Access Issue:
Attempts to check YARN logs returned:
Logs not available for container_1765880362887_0002_01_000001. Aggregation may not be completeReferences:
• Related OC incident: https://quantexa.atlassian.net/browse/DO-256

• Slack thread: https://quantexa.slack.com/archives/C06QE8VQZ6C/p1765891174389849

• Fusion discussion: https://quantexa.slack.com/archives/C0183KS4M62/p1765447631346069
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

tickets.summary: Improve DataOps Slack Workflow: clarity, traceability, and run visibility
tickets.description:
Following several months of active use of the DataOps Slack workflow (64 requests processed since mid-August, ~94% completion rate), we've gathered feedback from users across DEAD, QSB, Trials, and Data Feeds on how to improve clarity, consistency, and visibility as the workflow scales.
Overall feedback has been very positive, and the channel has become the central intake mechanism for DataOps requests. This ticket focuses on iterating the workflow template and downstream handling to address the main pain points raised, while keeping the process lightweight and easy to use.
Key themes from feedback:
_ Stronger clarity around _environment, data, and versioning*
_ Better visibility of _what exactly was run*, and where
_ Tighter linkage between the request and _execution outcomes*
• Reducing ambiguity for custom / non-standard runs (e.g. QSB)
This work should align with the longer-term direction of automating Slack → Airflow execution, but is valuable even independently as a usability and governance improvement.
Proposed Improvements (High Level)_ _Workflow form enhancements*
__ Make target environment (dev vs prod) explicit and unambiguous
__ Capture raw dataset details (e.g. custom datasets for QSB)
__ Require clearer versioning inputs:
__* Exact JAR version
__* Whether it is a custom build
__* Source branch for custom JARs (and expectation it remains stable)
__ NEW (Dec 2025 analysis): Add the following required fields:
__* One-off or Recurring (dropdown: One-off / Recurring)
__* Priority (dropdown: High within 2 days / Medium this week / Low flexible)
__* Requested completion date (date picker)
__ NEW: Add field validation/helper text:
__* data_id: "Use 'fixed' or 'latest' unless you have a specific date"
__* Iteration/Branch: "Case-sensitive: ER_Legacy ≠ ER_legacy"
__* Environment: Change from free text to dropdown (Ochre Dev / Ochre Prod)
_ _Request visibility & traceability*
__ Surface key run metadata directly on the request thread:
__* Environment
__* Data / dataset
__* Versions used
__* Iteration / run identifiers
__ Post success/failure notifications back into the originating request (not only the Airflow notification channel)
__ NEW: Create standardised "run card" response template for completed requests (Airflow link, output location, run ID, duration)
_ _Consistency & expectations*
__ Clarify expectations around what information must be final at request time
__ Reduce back-and-forth caused by missing or ambiguous details
__ Make it easier for DataOps to prioritise and reason about requests at a glance
_ _Foundations for future automation*
__ Structure request data so it can be reused cleanly by:
__* Slack → Airflow automation
__* Audit logging
__* Potential Jira ticket creation / tracking
Reference DocumentationNew guidelines published: DataOps Guidelines for Requesting ETL and Data Pipeline Support
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• The Slack workflow template explicitly captures environment, dataset, and versioning details with minimal ambiguity

• Each request thread clearly shows what was run (data, versions, env) and receives success/failure updates in-thread

• Feedback points from Milan, Tristan, and Ciaran are addressed in the updated workflow design

• Changes are reviewed with stakeholders and rolled out without disrupting existing usage patterns

tickets.summary: Productionise Slack → Airflow Automation (Phase 2)
tickets.description:
Following the successful prototype of the Slack → Airflow bridge, we now need to convert the POC into a production-grade system that can safely, reliably, and securely trigger ETL/BR pipelines from Slack across dev and prod environments.
The prototype has demonstrated full end-to-end functionality:
• Slack workflow ingestion and parameter extraction (legacy + new formats)

• Approval cards and threaded updates

• Run-card generation, DAG URLs, GCS paths, and Elasticsearch helpers

• kubectl-based Airflow triggering, including IAP tunnel orchestration

• A complete Airflow REST API client (trigger/list/status/tasks) with validation + cookie-based authentication

• Slack channel monitoring across public + private channels with full pagination

• Successful DAG triggering in ochre dev using real run_ids
This next phase focuses on turning the prototype into an operational service with proper authentication, RBAC, secrets handling, observability, and a safe, deterministic execution model aligned with R&D and Production Engineering best practices.
The goal is to deliver a fully productionised Slack → Airflow invocation layer that:
• Runs autonomously inside our cluster as a long-running, monitored service

• Uses supported authentication (service accounts / IAP / workload identity) rather than browser cookies

• Implements strict RBAC boundaries and full audit logging

• Supports dev + prod environments consistently

• Includes retry/backoff logic, error-handling, Slack rate-limit safety, and idempotent triggers

• Integrates cleanly with the existing Slack processor without changing the workflow experience
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Production-ready trigger mechanism: Slack approvals automatically trigger DAGs via a supported Airflow API/CLI integration.

• Security + validation: All DAG IDs and config keys pass strict allow-list validation with no unvalidated input paths.

• Observability + logging: All trigger attempts, approvals, payloads, and run_ids are logged and traceable across Slack and Airflow.

• Reliable behaviour: The service runs autonomously with retries, rate-limit handling, healthchecks, and stable behaviour across dev and prod environments.

tickets.summary: AI Tooling Rollout Across Applications (Pilot + Strategy)
tickets.description:
Define Pilot + Rollout Strategy for AI Tooling, Slash Commands, and claude.md Across Applications Repos.
Following discussions with Ed Hogarth, we need a centralised, scalable mechanism for rolling out AI tooling across Applications repos (including DEAD). Repo-by-repo manual updates are not viable. Based on spike QE-40258, two approaches are being evaluated: (1) Claude code hooks for auto-fetching tooling on startup, and (2) a Jenkins-based cascade that distributes slash commands and config to all repos.

This ticket covers mapping current coverage, selecting a pilot repo (data-packs or internal-data-feeds), validating the workflow, and defining the rollout plan for all Applications repos.

tickets.summary: Pre-emptible Node / Pipeline Reliability Guidance (saas-banking)
tickets.description: A set of reliability concerns were raised around the 3–4 hour Airflow-driven ETL → BR → GS → scoring pipeline in saas-banking, especially when run on pre-emptible nodes. A DevProd-aligned evaluation concluded that pipeline length and orchestration overhead—rather than node type—are the main bottlenecks. This ticket covers capturing the findings, documenting recommended improvements, and outlining the direction forward (e.g., shifting validation into Gradle with caching, using Airflow only for nightlies/investigation tests, and engaging Quantum Grease for retry logic where needed).
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Summarise key pipeline pain points (runtime, Airflow overhead, lack of caching)

• Document ownership boundaries: team-owned pipeline design vs. Quantum Grease retry mechanisms

• Provide clear DevProd-aligned guidance for next steps (short-term + long-term architecture direction)

• Share recommendations with saas-banking/QSB teams via #dead-dev-prod

• Ensure guidance becomes part of ongoing DevEx discussions across DEAD

tickets.summary: Enable Gradle Configuration Cache in standard-parsers (Plugin Fix Required)
tickets.description:
The migration towards enabling the Gradle configuration cache in standard-parsers has progressed through early steps (test-runner migration, plugin updates, removal of deprecated plugins, basic task rewrites). The remaining blockers all originate from the com.quantexa.plugins.model-download plugin, which currently prevents configuration-cache compatibility due to disallowed behaviours (Project references + afterTask listeners).

This work involves moving the remaining fixes upstream into the quantexa-gradle-plugins/model-download plugin, creating a local snapshot for validation, then releasing a new plugin version for consumption by standard-parsers.

tickets.summary: Manual OS Subset Creation for Transient ES Cluster (One-Off)
tickets.description:
Create a one-off 1000-record OpenSanctions subset to support the QSB transient ES workflow for the saas-banking project, due to 5 GB disk limitations on the transient Elasticsearch pod.
Background & ContextA DataOps workflow request was raised to run an ETL Full for OpenSanctions from the latest IDF main branch (PR #1146).
The initial full ETL run completed successfully, and the indices + snapshots were produced, but the resulting indices were too large to be restored into the transient ES cluster used by QSB due to the 5 GB disk limit.
QSB requires a ~1000 record subset of OpenSanctions for transient bootstrapping.
However:
• OS and ICIJ are the only two datasources that do not currently have a raw-data subset stage in Data Feeds ingest (subsetRatio = 1 by default).

• Therefore, no pre-existing subset exists for OS.

• The subset must be derived manually from ETL outputs.
After discussion between QSB, DataOps, and Data Feeds, the agreed solution for this request is:
Scope of Work (Manual One-Off)• Read the latest OS ETL output:
gs://quantexa-ochre-data-lake-opensanctions-dev/processed/etl/iterations/2.7_2.x_1.x-real-msb-1288/opensanctions/<run>/Compounds/DocumentIndexInput.parquet• Take the first 1000 records and write a reduced version of the dataset.

• Use the reduced dataset to:
• Regenerate resolver and search indices.

• Generate transient-ready snapshots following the naming pattern:
opensanctions-2.7_2.x_1.x-real-msb-1288_<YYYYMMDDHHMMSS>
• Provide the output index names + snapshot name back to QSB so they can restore into the transient ES cluster.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• A 1000-record subset of OS is successfully produced.

• All OS indices (resolver/search) are regenerated using the subset.

• New snapshots are created in:
quantexa-ochre-data-lake-opensanctions-es-subset
• Snapshot name is shared with QSB.

tickets.summary: DevProd
tickets.description:
This epic tracks all ongoing Developer Productivity work across the DEAD area and the wider Applications group. It provides a single place to coordinate, prioritise, and deliver improvements that streamline developer workflows, reduce build and pipeline friction, and introduce consistent tooling across repos.
The initial focus includes three foundational strands of work:
• Build & CI enhancements — starting with configuration-cache enablement in key repos and upstream plugin fixes that unlock faster Gradle builds.

• Pipeline reliability & DevEx guidance — documenting issues such as long-running Airflow pipelines on pre-emptible nodes and steering teams toward more scalable, cacheable patterns.

• AI tooling rollout across DEAD — establishing a centralised, sustainable mechanism for distributing slash commands, Claude configuration, and repo-specific guidance (aligned with QE-40258).
These strands are only the start.
As the DevProd liaison role evolves, additional workstreams, improvements, and cross-team initiatives will be added under this epic, ensuring everything is tracked, visible, and consistently delivered across the Applications landscape.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

tickets.summary: DataOps Support
tickets.description:
A central place to track all manual, ad-hoc, or one-off operational tasks carried out by DataOps across R&D. This includes transient environment fixes, manual subsets, bespoke reruns, direct snapshot manipulation, index repairs, and any short-term work requested outside the standard automated pipelines.
Epic DescriptionThe DataOps team routinely handles a wide range of support interventions that fall outside the automated DataOps and Data Feeds workflows — such as regenerating indices, creating manual subsets, performing special ETL reruns, resolving transient ES issues, or directly manipulating snapshot repositories. These tasks are often necessary to unblock downstream teams (QSB, Data Feeds, Trials, Analytics, etc.) but they consume engineering capacity and frequently highlight underlying gaps in process, automation, or platform features.
This epic exists to:
• Track all manual DataOps work in a single place.

• Quantify the operational load caused by bespoke or non-standard activities.

• Identify recurring patterns that indicate the need for new automation, workflow improvements, or upstream changes.

• Surface reliability and process gaps that increase manual toil.

• Provide visibility to R&D leadership on the hidden operational costs behind maintaining data reliability and transient environments.
Over time, the aggregated tickets under this epic will help determine:
• Whether existing processes should be standardised or consolidated.

• Whether new DataOps automation should be introduced.

• Whether specific datasources (e.g., OS/ICIJ) require dedicated subset stages or pipeline enhancements.

• How to reduce manual intervention and move toward more self-service and self-healing pipelines.
OutcomeA clear view of the manual operational burden in DataOps, enabling data-driven decisions on where to invest in automation, redesign workflows, or evolve platform capabilities.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

tickets.summary: Refactor & Centralise Airflow JAR Handling to Include All Required Dependencies
tickets.description:
Tristan highlighted that our Airflow JAR-handling logic is fragmented and overdue for consolidation. The recent ClassNotFoundException for QuantexaSparkScriptRunner exposed that we’re only attaching the Batch Resolver shadow JAR, while other required shadow JARs (e.g. Spark/Elastic script-runner JARs used in Demo Banking) are not being included.
This ticket covers reviewing how all JARs are discovered, selected, and passed to Spark, and refactoring the Python code so that JAR handling lives in one place, is consistent across pipelines, and reliably includes all required components.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• AC1: Identify the complete set of JARs needed for Batch Resolver flows (including script-runner and Spark/Elastic shadow JARs).

• AC2: Centralise JAR-handling logic into a single module/function used by all Airflow tasks.

• AC3: Ensure Airflow reliably attaches all required JARs when submitting Spark jobs.

tickets.summary: Enable Jenkins pipeline to upload shadow JARs to GCS instead of Nexus for snapshots
tickets.description:
We need to modify or create a new Jenkinsfile under
internal-data-feeds/cicd/internal-data-feeds-features/
so that snapshot builds upload shadow JARs to a GCS bucket rather than Nexus.
This is required because Nexus snapshot artefacts cannot currently be resolved properly. When a snapshot is published, Gradle/Ivy attempts to resolve a pom rather than the actual snapshot artefact, resulting in dependency resolution failures in Ochre environments.
This issue — originally raised years ago and discussed again in June — remains unresolved. As confirmed by Dave and others, we lack the necessary admin access or configuration to fix snapshot resolution within the Nexus snapshot repository.
As a result, developers cannot reliably use snapshot builds for testing custom JARs. Uploading the shadow JARs directly to a GCS bucket provides a lightweight, permission-free workaround that integrates easily with Data Feeds dev testing and the Entity Quality workflows.
The pipeline should:
• Run the Gradle command:
./gradlew -p data-source-all projectShadowJar dependencyShadowJar --refresh-dependencies --no-build-cache --parallel

tickets.summary: Enable Jenkins pipeline to upload shadow JARs to GCS instead of Nexus for snapshots
tickets.description:
We need to modify or create a new Jenkinsfile under
internal-data-feeds/cicd/internal-data-feeds-features/
so that snapshot builds upload shadow JARs to a GCS bucket rather than Nexus.
This is required because Nexus snapshot artefacts cannot currently be resolved properly. When a snapshot is published, Gradle/Ivy attempts to resolve a pom rather than the actual snapshot artefact, resulting in dependency resolution failures in Ochre environments.
This issue — originally raised years ago and discussed again in June — remains unresolved. As confirmed by Dave and others, we lack the necessary admin access or configuration to fix snapshot resolution within the Nexus snapshot repository.
As a result, developers cannot reliably use snapshot builds for testing custom JARs. Uploading the shadow JARs directly to a GCS bucket provides a lightweight, permission-free workaround that integrates easily with Data Feeds dev testing and the Entity Quality workflows.
The pipeline should:
• Run the Gradle command:
./gradlew -p data-source-all projectShadowJar dependencyShadowJar --refresh-dependencies --no-build-cache --parallel
• Upload the generated JARs to:
• gs://quantexa-ochre-1-dead-dev-1-config/jars/ (default)

• or optionally gs://quantexa-ochre-1-dead-dev-1-cicd/jars/

• Use consistent naming:
• data-source-all-shadow-dependency_2.12-<jar_version>.jar

• data-source-all-shadow-projects_2.12-<jar_version>.jar
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Jenkins pipeline parameterised with JAR_VERSION, BRANCH, and GCS_BUCKET(??).

• Builds shadow JARs using the specified Gradle tasks.

• Uploads both JARs to the defined GCS bucket.

• Verifies uploaded files exist via gsutil ls.

tickets.summary: Orbis Extract Compounds Failing on 2.7.7_1.3 Due to directorId Null Values
tickets.description:
Running Orbis (2.7.7_1.3) on Spark 3.5 failed during the extract compounds stage with:
Caused by: com.quantexa.etl.spark.TransformationRunnerException: Exception thrown while processing row with documentID GB10419610  
Caused by: java.lang.NullPointerExceptionInvestigation shows directorId values are occasionally null, causing downstream joins and recordId generation to fail.
This mirrors the previous issue tracked under DF-1931 (BVD Document coming through as Array[null] instead of []), which was closed as non-reproducible. However, the issue has now been replicated on Orbis using May data, even though logic hasn’t changed since 2024.
Reference: data-packs commit 8d5ea29 (from 2023) which introduced handling for null directorId.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
•  Reproduce the failure using Orbis 2.7.7_1.3 with May data.

•  Confirm root cause (directorId null propagation during join).

•  Apply / backport logic from commit 8d5ea29 to handle null directorId.

•  Verify successful extract compounds run with no NPE or write failures.

•  Confirm behaviour aligns with fixed handling in newer branches (e.g. 4.2 parsers).

tickets.summary: [BUG] Elastic snapshot task double-runs; second attempt hits repository_conflict_exception
tickets.description:
Elastic snapshot stage appears to execute twice; the second attempt sees an in-progress snapshot and fails the whole stage (affects multiple datasources intermittently). Error sample:
Java.lang.RuntimeException: Error type: repository_conflict_exception, reason: [dea-development] trying to modify or unregister repository that is currently used (snapshot is in progress)
Root error type: repository_conflict_exception, reason: [dea-development] trying to modify or unregister repository that is currently used (snapshot is in progress)
  at com.quantexa.elastic.client.Elastic8Client.process(Elastic8Client.scala:793)
  at com.quantexa.elastic.client.Elastic8Client.$anonfun$createRepository$3(Elastic8Client.scala:884)
  ...
  at com.quantexa.etl.scripts.elasticsearch.snapshot.AbstractCreateSnapshotScript.run(AbstractCreateSnapshotScript.scala:79)
Likely causes: duplicate trigger (retries/concurrency), missing idempotency/locking around repo creation & snapshot start, or insufficient backoff on repository_conflict_exception.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Snapshot stage executes once per run (respecting Airflow task concurrency/queueing) with idempotency guard (e.g., “snapshot in progress” check) before repository changes.

• On repository_conflict_exception, task retries with exponential backoff + jitter and does not fail the stage; when a snapshot is already in progress, the task waits then resumes.

Observed: the First Failed Task (import_raw.dataproc_logger) does not correspond to the Dataproc Job ID (replication_elastic__142f22c86). Likely root causes: wrong TI selected (e.g., last vs first failure), cross-task XCom lookup, or non-atomic collection when multiple tasks fail close together.

tickets.summary: ClassNotFoundException bug with SparkMeasure on ETL scripts (works in Airflow)
tickets.description:
Several ETL jobs executed via batch scripts are failing due to missing FlightRecorderStageMetrics class from SparkMeasure. This does not occur when running the same jobs orchestrated via Airflow, where the correct configs are applied.
The issue appears to be:
• ETL scripts are missing spark.executor.extraClassPath (present in Airflow configs).

• Jobs that include SparkMeasure fail immediately when registering the SparkListener.

• Temporary workaround has been to remove SparkMeasure settings from ETL configs, but this disables performance metrics collection.
We need to align ETL script configs with Airflow configs (extra classpath & JAR settings) to ensure Flight Recorder is available and metrics can be collected consistently.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• ETL scripts include correct SparkMeasure JAR configs (matching Airflow setup).

• ETL jobs run successfully with SparkMeasure enabled (no ClassNotFoundException).

tickets.summary: Add VM labels to identify datasource, etl stage etc [0pts]
tickets.description:
Currently, Dataproc VMs lack metadata labels that would help identify what datasource
and ETL stage they're processing. This makes it difficult to:
• Track costs per datasource

• Debug issues by identifying which stage is running

• Filter/search VMs by datasource or stage

• Set up resource quotas per datasource

• Improve observability and monitoring
We should add labels to Dataproc clusters when they're created to include:
• Datasource (e.g., opensanctions, orbis, dnb)

• Pipeline name

• ETL stage name

• Environment (dev/prod)

• Iteration/version

• Owner/team
This will improve cost tracking, debugging, and overall observability of our ETL infrastructure.
tickets.creator: Tristan Dickson
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• VM labels are added when creating Dataproc clusters

• Labels include: datasource, pipeline, stage, environment, iteration

• Labels follow GCP naming conventions (lowercase, hyphens, max 63 chars)

• Labels are visible in GCP Console under VM instances

• Cost reports can be filtered by datasource label

• Documentation updated with label naming conventions

tickets.summary: Design / Feasibility: Repo Split for Scala Product vs Python ETL/Airflow
tickets.description:
Last year a proposal was made to split Scala product code and Python ETL/Airflow code into separate repositories. With DataOps expanding and the “Package Airflow Modules as a Product” epic underway, this idea is being revisited.
The goal is to:
• Keep internal-data-feeds focused on Scala product code, Gradle, and product CI/CD.

• Move Python ETL/orchestration (Airflow DAGs, Python modules, configs, tests, promotion pipelines) into a new repo (name TBD, likely DataOps-focused).
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

parent.summary: Automated Incremental ETL Framework for Scheduled Live ETL refreshes

tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Touchpoints mapped — Document where Scala and Python currently overlap (e.g. .conf layering, DAG/jar coupling).

• Ownership boundaries defined — Decide which repo owns configs, DAGs, jars, and promotion pipelines.

• Generic vs feed-specific Python separated — Identify which modules (e.g. quantexa_airflow_helpers) can be packaged as reusable “QSS Airflow” libraries, and which remain tied to feed-specific jars.

• Config-agnostic feasibility assessed — Explore whether the Python code can be made flexible enough to run across multiple implementation repos.

• Company impact articulated — Capture concrete examples of incidents and time lost due to the current coupling (e.g. blocked ETL changes forcing product releases, conf mismatches, jar version drift). Show how the split would have mitigated those.

• Repo structure proposed — Recommend new repo structure, naming options, and versioning approach.

tickets.summary: Implement Comparison Grandparent DAG (Multi-Version Orchestration)
tickets.description:
User story: As an ERQ tester, I want to run ETL + BR jobs across multiple parsers/versions in parallel, so I can compare Post-BR outputs automatically and evaluate parser upgrades.
For parser upgrades (e.g. 3.1 vs 4.3), we need a way to run ETL + BR jobs for the same datasources under different parsers in parallel and then compare Post-BR outputs.
Scope:
• Define a “grandparent DAG” orchestrating multiple child DAGs.

• Support multiple parser versions from a single Slack request.

• Trigger ETL + BR for each branch.

• Run Post-BR comparison tasks (BuildDiff, EQM).

• Publish results (dashboards/reports) to GCS + Slack.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Single Slack request can trigger multi-version ETL + BR runs.

• Each branch produces distinct outputs, linked in the run registry.

• Post-BR comparison artifacts (dashboards, diffs) are generated and accessible.

tickets.summary: Surface Run Configs as “Run Cards” for All Batch Ops
tickets.description:
User story: As a user debugging ETL/BR runs, I want a surfaced “Run Card” showing the exact parameters and config used, so I don’t need to hunt through Airflow logs to understand what happened.
Debugging is slowed because runtime parameters and configs are buried in logs. Introduce a standard “Run Card” artifact for every run.
Scope:
• Capture datasources, jar version, parser version, iteration, cluster profile, and resolver config snapshot.

• Generate a Run Card (JSON + Markdown) per run.

• Post Run Card link to Slack via the approval bot.

• Store Run Card in GCS and link it in the run registry/JIRA.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• 100% of runs produce a Run Card artifact.

• Slack messages include Run Card links.

tickets.summary: Migrate Resolver Config from Trials to Internal Data Feeds
tickets.description:
User story: As a DataOps owner, I want resolver configs migrated into the Internal Data Feeds repo, so that ownership is centralised, duplication is removed, and configs are aligned for production.
The “generic” resolver config currently lives in Trials, creating duplication and drift. Ownership should move to Internal Data Feeds.
Scope:
• Copy baseline resolver config templates into Internal Data Feeds repo.

• Align DAGs to reference configs from Internal Data Feeds location.

• Deprecate and remove Trials references.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• 100% of DAGs reference resolver configs from Internal Data Feeds.

• No residual dependencies on Trials.

• Config logic centralised and maintained in Internal Data Feeds.

tickets.summary: Implement Resolver Config Version Control
tickets.description:
User story: As a user running BR jobs, I want resolver configs to be version-controlled and reproducible, so that I can re-run or debug any past job with confidence and no config drift.
Resolver configs today are mutable and not tracked, leading to drift and unreproducible runs. Introduce version control tied to iteration, parser version, and jar version.
Scope:
• Define versioning scheme (e.g. iteration|parser|jar).

• Store configs as immutable snapshots in GCS + Git (if feasible).

• Link resolver config snapshots to each DAG run in the run registry.

• Provide diff view (Slack/Git) for debugging changes.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Any past BR run can be reproduced by fetching its config snapshot.

• Config diffs are visible and attributable.

tickets.summary: Self‑Service Batch Operations (DataOps)
tickets.description:
VisionProvide a self-service batch operations platform where ETL → Batch Resolver (BR) → Post-BR quality checks can be safely requested and run through a Slack workflow with approval, Airflow execution, and automated feedback. Users get fast, reliable runs with surfaced configs, metrics, and results, while DataOps retains control through approvals, guardrails, and versioning.
Why Now• Current manual triggers and fragmented patterns create delays, risks, and inconsistency.

• Lack of automation for run configs, resolver config versioning, and feedback loops makes reproducibility harder.

• R&D teams (Analytics, EQ, Trials) need safe, structured self-service to accelerate iteration without overloading DataOps.
Headline Outcomes (KPIs)• ⬇️ Median request-to-run time < 2 minutes after approval.

• ⬆️ ≥ 95% successful self-service runs.

• 📈 ≥ 70% adoption within 60 days (majority of ETL/BR/Post-BR requests via self-service).

• ⬇️ Debug time reduced by 50% via surfaced configs and metric locations.

• ⬇️ Config drift eliminated through resolver config versioning and centralisation.
SummaryA unified, approval-driven self-service system for batch operations — requested via Slack workflow, approved by DataOps, executed via Airflow, and automatically reporting configs, GCS outputs, and metrics back to the user. As R&D, we need a single, guard-railed process to run ETL, BR, and Post-BR jobs without waiting for manual intervention.
Scope (What This Epic Will Deliver)This epic covers 11 tickets grouped into phases:
Phase 1 — Core Foundations
• EQ-119 Dynamic BR Config Generation (multi-datasource).

• EQ-136 Implement Resolver Config Version Control.

• EQ-137 Migrate Resolver Config from Trials → Internal Data Feeds.

• EQ-138 Surface Run Configs as “Run Cards” for All Batch Ops.

• EQ-140 Prototype Slack → Airflow Bridge with Approval Flow.
Phase 2 — Workflow Enhancements & Feedback
• EQ-141 Slack Workflow UX Improvements & Input Validation.

• EQ-142 Approval Flow & Audit Logging Enhancements.

• EQ-143 Failure Feedback with Metrics, Logs & Runbook Links.
Phase 3 — Registry & Orchestration
• EQ-144 Integrate Run Registry for Self-Service Batch Operations.

• EQ-139 Implement Comparison Grandparent DAG (multi-version orchestration).
Phase 4 — Adoption & Rollout
• EQ-145 Adoption Rollout, Training & Confluence Documentation.
How It Works• Slack Workflow as the Front Door
• Users select datasources, jar version, iteration, and run type (ETL, BR, or both).

• Input validation ensures only supported datasources, jars, and other config are accepted.

• Approval Gate
• Requests routed to DataOps approver.

• On approval, job triggered in Airflow.

• Full audit trail: requested_by, approved_by, dag_id, dag_run_id.

• Airflow Execution & Bot Feedback
• Slack bot posts back the exact run config (“Run Card”), GCS output path, and surfaced metrics/logs.

• On failure, bot provides:
• Diagnostic messages (infra/data/code class).

• Links to Spark Flight Recorder metrics + Airflow logs.

• Runbook guidance for self-diagnosis.

• Config Management Improvements
• Resolver configs version-controlled (iteration/jar/parser).

• Generic version migrated to Internal Data Feeds repo.

• BR configs dynamically generated for any datasource combination (multi-datasource support).

• Advanced Orchestration
• Grandparent DAG orchestrates multiple ETL/BR runs in parallel.

• Supports multi-version comparisons (e.g. parser 3.1 vs 4.3).

• Enables Post-BR comparison after both runs complete.

• Workflow Enhancements & Feedback
• Validated inputs prevent invalid jobs.

• Approval and audit metadata surfaced in Slack.

• Run configs presented consistently for easier debugging.

• Registry & Adoption
• Run Registry integration ensures every run has a permanent record of configs, metrics, and outputs.

• Confluence docs, live demos, and training sessions drive adoption.

• Target: ≥70% of requests routed through Slack workflow within 60 days.

tickets.summary: Prototype Slack → Airflow Bridge with Approval Flow
tickets.description:
User Story: As a user requesting an ETL/BR run, I want to submit my parameters through Slack and receive clear approval and feedback, so that I can safely trigger jobs without navigating the Airflow UI and still see the exact configs, run status, and outputs.
I’m building a Slack → Airflow bridge to streamline the trigger process for ETL jobs and reduce manual, error-prone steps.
Currently, users submit a Slack workflow form with parameters such as run type, iteration, JAR version, and parser version. The proposed solution will automatically generate an Airflow trigger payload from these inputs and send me a Slack DM with an approval card showing the exact trigger details.
If approved, the job will be triggered automatically, and the result will be posted back into the channel, including the run status and a direct link to the DAG. This keeps full visibility for all stakeholders while eliminating the need to manually navigate the Airflow UI.
The system will also support advanced scenarios, such as resuming from a specific stage or applying input overrides, using validated templates to ensure consistency and accuracy.
This proof of concept has already been successfully demonstrated in Ochre dev using the Airflow CLI. The next phase is to move to the Airflow REST API, with RBAC and a strict allow list of configuration keys for security. If CLI execution is still required, it will be routed through a locked-down runner with full audit logging.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Slack workflow form submission generates a pre-constructed Airflow trigger payload.

• An approval card is sent via Slack DM showing the exact command or API body before execution.

• On approval, the job is triggered automatically using the Airflow REST API (preferred) or CLI runner (fallback).

• The triggering event is posted back into the original channel, including run status and a direct link to the DAG run.

• All requests are validated against an allow-list of approved DAGs and configuration keys for security and auditability.

• Correct DAG URLs

• Correct Elastic index lookup commands

• Approval/rejection flow works with interactive responses.

• Successfully tested in #dataops-test — the bot can ingest workflow requests, parse parameters, format responses, and post threaded updates exactly as expected.

• The approval step currently generates the full Airflow trigger payload (run_id, iteration, datasource, jar_version, etc.) exactly as the final system would require.

• The next piece was prototyped using the Airflow CLI locally: I can manually trigger the exact DAG from my laptop after the Slack approval (using kubectl exec into the Airflow worker pod).
What’s left / not done yet:
• The Slack bot cannot yet trigger Airflow directly — this final action is still a manual step for me.

• Need to implement the actual DAG trigger step via:
• Airflow REST API (preferred), or

• A locked-down CLI runner in the cluster as a fallback.

• Need to introduce:
• RBAC validation

• Allow-list of DAGs and config keys

• Auditing/logging for all trigger events

• Some additional testing across unusual parameter combinations (resume points, overrides, custom jar versions).

• Integration into a production-safe environment (token handling, secrets, namespace routing).

• For now, the POC can generate the Airflow payload and approval UI, but the execution itself still requires me to run the trigger manually using kubectl.
Estimate remaining: ~3–4 points to finish the Airflow trigger integration, harden the validation, and wrap it into a fully automated Slack → Airflow flow.
Happy to continue when bandwidth allows — the core Slack side is solid, and the remaining work is focused entirely on the Airflow invocation layer.

I’ve made significant end-to-end progress on the Slack → Airflow automation. • Handles SSL options, error reporting, and Airflow UI URL generation

Together, this gives us a fully working Slack → Airflow execution path via kubectl today, and a complete REST API client ready for eventual service-account or platform-supported access.

tickets.summary: Redesign Dataproc Logger Stage to Reduce Logging API Overuse [1pts]
tickets.description:
The current dataproc_logger operator is too aggressive in its use of the Cloud Logging API. Every ETL stage executes multiple wide queries (cluster-level events, jobId searches, YARN application logs, keyword scans), regardless of job outcome. With many concurrent ETLs running, this pushes the project against the logging.googleapis.com/read_requests quota (60 requests/min/project), leading to RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED errors.
Problem:
• Even successful jobs trigger multiple cluster- and job-wide log queries.

• Broad filters (cluster UUID + general searches) increase API calls unnecessarily.

• Failures in log collection generate noisy errors in Airflow, obscuring real issues.
Proposed Changes:
• Lightweight path for successful jobs
• Fetch and save Dataproc job status via jobs.get.

• Run only a minimal, jobId-scoped log query.

• Publish Log Explorer URL(s) with baked filters to XCom/logs instead of pulling full payloads.

• Verbose path for failed jobs
• If job state = ERROR, then trigger full YARN and keyword searches.

• Retain deep collection of cluster logs, stderr, TaskSetManager warnings, and keyword scans, but only when needed.

• Query scoping and batching
• Replace broad cluster-UUID queries with narrow jobId-specific filters.

• Batch multiple resource filters into fewer entries.list calls (up to 100 supported).

• Rate limiting and backoff
• Add retry logic with exponential backoff for 429 RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED.

• Optionally introduce jitter/sleep to smooth bursts of concurrent runs.

• Optional enhancements
• Provide prebaked Log Explorer URLs alongside saved logs.

• Explore exporting logs to GCS/BigQuery for heavy offline reads instead of repeated API calls.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Successful jobs trigger ≤2 Logging API calls (Dataproc job status + narrow jobId logs).

• Failed jobs still collect full diagnostic logs (YARN/cluster/keyword searches).

• Airflow tasks no longer hit the logging.googleapis.com/read_requests quota during concurrent ETL runs.

• Log Explorer URLs are surfaced in task output or XCom.

• Logger code is refactored, documented, and tested across subset and full ETL DAGs.

• Sign off from Ventsi

tickets.summary: Airflow2 Scheduler Pod Unavailable – CICD Deployments Failing
tickets.description:
Our nightly CICD pipeline for internal data feeds is consistently failing at the stage that promotes DAGs to Airflow. The failure occurs because no running Airflow scheduler pod is found in namespace airflow2.
Evidence from Jenkins logs:
ERROR: No running Airflow scheduler pod found in namespace airflow2When replicated locally with kubectl, connection to the Kubernetes API server fails due to TLS certificate verification issues:
Unable to connect to the server: tls: failed to verify certificate: x509: certificate signed by unknown authorityThis issue is not isolated to the nightly run — it also affects feature branch deployments. As a result, Airflow DAG promotion fails before variables are updated, blocking developers from testing or deploying.
The broken state in dev is confirmed by multiple engineers.
It appears to be a Kubernetes-level problem with the airflow2 pod and its scheduler rather than a DAG code issue.
Linked qcp support ticket:

tickets.summary: Fix OpenSanctions ETL validation failures by expanding schema whitelist
tickets.description:
The OpenSanctions ETL validation stage has been failing since week 2025-34 on both dev and prod environments, blocking data ingestion with the following error:
java.lang.IllegalStateException: The following validations failed: Row Validation for the datasource OpenSanctions
[Validation Failed: openSanctionsListedSchema, Failures: 6336, Reason: Fields failed: schema]
[Validation Failed: OpenSanctionsOrganizationDatesRule, Failures: 1211, Reason: Fields failed: incorporationDate, dissolutionDate]Root Cause AnalysisComprehensive analysis of weeks 2025-32, 2025-33, and 2025-34 reveals:
• Schema Validation Explosion: OpenSanctions introduced new entity types not in our validation whitelist:
• Documentation: +3,835 failures

• Article: +2,289 failures  

• PublicBody: +153 failures

• Asset: +35 failures

• Payment: +22 failures

• Debt: +2 failures

• Validation Threshold Breach: Total failure rate increased from 0.0721% (week 2025-33) to 0.1805% (week 2025-34), exceeding the configured 0.1% threshold (validationThreshold = 0.999)

• Progressive Degradation: Schema failures grew from 210 (2025-32) → 1,793 (2025-33) → 6,336 (2025-34)
Business Impact• ETL Pipeline Down: Both nightly and incremental runs failing

• Data Freshness: Week 2025-34 data cannot be ingested

• Environments Affected: Dev and Prod

• Revenue Impact: Potential downstream system outages
SolutionExpand the schema validation whitelist in OpenSanctionsValidationUtils.scala to include the new schema types introduced by OpenSanctions.
Files Modified• data-source-opensanctions/opensanctions-ingest/src/main/scala/com/quantexa/opensanctions/ingest/utils/OpenSanctionsValidationUtils.scala

tickets.summary: Airflow stage marked as success when job is cancelled
tickets.description:
We have identified an issue where Airflow marks a stage as SUCCESS on a retry even when the underlying Dataproc job was actually CANCELLED (e.g., due to a timeout). This behaviour is not limited to a specific stage — it can occur on any stage in the pipeline, such as cleanse, extract compounds, etc.
Example:
[2025-08-06, 14:43:10 UTC] {quantexa_dataproc_operators.py:228} INFO - Match found with labels task_id cleansespark-submit and run_id manual--2025-08-06t0933180000. Current job state: CANCELLED
[2025-08-06, 14:43:10 UTC] {taskinstance.py:1398} INFO - Marking task as SUCCESS. dag_id=etl_orbis_csv_full_er_testing, task_id=cleanse.spark_submit, execution_date=20250806T093318, start_date=20250806T144309, end_date=20250806T144310Impact:
• Failures can go undetected if Airflow marks cancelled jobs as successful.

• Delays in root cause analysis due to inaccurate task state.

• Increased risk of data quality issues passing unnoticed.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Detect when a Dataproc job was CANCELLED and ensure the Airflow task is marked as FAILED rather than SUCCESS.

• Apply the fix in the relevant operator(s) so it works consistently for all stages.

• Add logging to clearly indicate when a retry skips execution due to an already cancelled job.

• Test with simulated timeouts/cancellations to verify correct task status is set.

tickets.summary: [SPIKE] Infrastructure-Level Investigation of Dataproc Executor Loss and Node Failures
tickets.description:
Business Justification:
Frequent executor and node failures on Dataproc are causing unpredictable job crashes across multiple ETL pipelines, impacting data delivery and stakeholder trust. This spike is essential to diagnose infrastructure-level root causes and ensure reliable, cost-effective Spark processing at scale.
This spike is focused on a thorough investigation of intermittent Dataproc stability issues significantly impacting Data Feeds ETL and Batch Resolver workflows. 
The primary symptoms include:
• Frequent executor/container losses (Exit code -100 and Exit code 143), resulting in abrupt Spark stage/job failures.

• Failures occurring simultaneously across multiple independent clusters and DAGs, suggesting infrastructure-level root causes rather than isolated Spark resource issues.
Usman now has enhanced access as a project-admin allowing:
• SSH access to Dataproc cluster master and worker nodes provisioned via Airflow.

• Direct access to Dataproc component web interfaces (e.g., YARN Resource Manager, Spark History Server) enabled via the Component Gateway feature.

• Access to Stackdriver logs, Cloud Monitoring dashboards, and VM-level system logs (dmesg, syslog, messages) to perform in-depth infrastructure diagnostics.
The investigation will specifically aim to:
• Confirm patterns of node loss and container termination within Dataproc infrastructure (networking, node eviction, resource exhaustion).

• Correlate Spark-level errors and JVM metrics (flight recorder data) with infrastructure-level logs and metrics.

• Analyze historical Spark job configurations, cluster resource allocations, and recent changes (if any) to isolate factors contributing to these stability issues.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

parent.summary: Automated Incremental ETL Framework for Scheduled Live ETL refreshes

tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Produce a detailed diagnostic report outlining clear findings about the causes and frequency of Dataproc stability issues.

• Deliver targeted recommendations to DevOps/HAT addressing root cause infrastructure issues, including suggested configuration adjustments, resource management, or stability improvements.

• If direct action by DevOps/HAT is delayed or declined, provide an alternative mitigation or robust retry strategy to improve immediate workflow stability internally

tickets.summary: [SPIKE] Root Cause Analysis of Missing Airflow Logs and GCS Log Upload Failures
tickets.description:
Business Justification:
Unreliable Airflow logging is undermining observability and incident resolution across Data Feeds, causing delays in diagnosing task failures and risking missed SLAs. This spike enables us to identify the root causes and unblock automation by restoring stable, end-to-end visibility of ETL workflows.
This spike aims to comprehensively investigate and document the ongoing Airflow log flakiness issue impacting reliability and automated ETL workflows in Data Feeds. 
Logs frequently go missing or become inaccessible in Google Cloud Storage (GCS), with errors including:
• [Errno -2] Name or service not known

• 404 NOT FOUND

• Malformed URLs

• Logs not flushing due to abrupt worker termination
These problems result in:
• Difficulty debugging failed tasks (missing logs)

• Misleading Airflow UI statuses (tasks stuck in "scheduled" or "queued" states)

• Silent workflow failures with reduced observability and unstable auto-alerting
The initial investigation will utilize a Kubernetes-based script (provided by Ventsi) to:
• Extract comprehensive logs and metadata directly from the Airflow scheduler pods

• Identify correlations between scheduler logs and missing or inaccessible logs

• Quantify the frequency and patterns of occurrences (time-based, DAG-based, or task-based correlations)
Usman has been granted permissions to:
• Execute kubectl commands directly within the Airflow Kubernetes namespace (airflow2), specifically targeting scheduler and worker pods.

• Access GCS buckets (quantexa-ochre-1-dead-dev-1-airflow2-logs) to retrieve and analyze stored log files.

• Run specialized scripts (provided by Ventsi) for systematic log extraction and analysis.
The goal of this spike is to:
• Confirm or rule out infrastructure-level issues versus Airflow/Celery application-level issues.

• Identify actionable steps for mitigation, including potential scheduler resource adjustments, retry logic enhancements, or logging configuration changes.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Produce a detailed report summarizing findings from log analysis, clearly indicating frequency, pattern recognition, and suspected root causes.

• Provide a clear, actionable set of recommendations for DevOps/HAT to remediate, or establish a documented plan for addressing these issues internally.

tickets.summary: Package Airflow Modules as a Product
tickets.description:
Business Impact: Accelerates developer productivity, ensures consistency, and reduces duplicated effort across Quantexa projects by providing well-tested, standardized Airflow components as reusable modules.
Description: Refactor and package core Airflow utilities into clearly documented, versioned Python packages, facilitating seamless reuse across projects and alignment with infrastructure constraints. Target modules from the /airflow/dags/modules directory include:
• data_download_scripts: Handles data acquisition from providers (D&B, Dowjones, GRID) through FTP/SFTP, encryption handling, and Moody's DataHub connectors.

• data_ingest_helpers: Provides DAG construction via build_data_download_dag(), dynamically managing VMs, SSH connections, provider-specific scripts execution, and cleanup operations with robust error handling.

• dynamic_dag_helpers: Implements config-driven DAG generation through ConfigLoader for optimal Spark settings and environment-specific parameters, and DAGCreator for intelligent workflow generation with dependency resolution and retry logic.

• quantexa_airflow_helpers: Manages Dataproc cluster lifecycle and Spark jobs through QuantexaDataprocSubmitJobOperator (job idempotency, recovery, JAR repository management) and QuantexaDataprocClusterCreateOperator (custom YARN/Spark optimization, SSD configuration, multi-cloud support).

• logging_helpers: Provides comprehensive logging via GCPBucketLogHandler for real-time Cloud Storage log streaming, and sophisticated ETLLogs class for post-execution analysis, intelligent filtering, regex-based YARN ID extraction, structured log organization, and targeted error diagnostics.
Key Deliverables:
• Modularize and refactor Airflow utilities into structured Python packages.

• Provide thorough documentation, configuration guidelines, and example use cases.

• Engage stakeholders (DevOps, SaaS Banking, OFX/GTR) for iterative feedback.

• Align with infrastructure and dependency constraints set by DevOps.
Success Metrics:
• Widespread adoption across Quantexa internal projects.

• Reduced boilerplate code by at least 40%.

• Decreased DAG setup and onboarding time by at least 25%.
Stakeholders: DevOps, SaaS Banking, OFX/GTR, Internal Airflow Users

tickets.summary: Intermittent Dataproc Executor Failures Causing ETL & BR Job Instability in Airflow
tickets.description:
We are experiencing frequent and unexplained failures across multiple Spark stages in our Dataproc-based ETL and Batch Resolver (BR) jobs. These failures manifest as lost executors, containers marked as failed on lost nodes, and abnormal Spark shutdowns. The issue affects critical components such as:
• ETL Extract Compounds Job
• Example Job ID: extract_compounds__bd64765f2

• Failures due to containers being released on lost nodes (Exit code -100)

• Executors lost in batches on the same host node (e.g. executor 495–513 on airflow-dpr-er-testing-mega-n2-dnb-incr-sw-f2hx)

• Batch Resolver Compound Enrichment Stage
• Job ID: compound-enrichment__b1d5127f1

• Exit code 143 (Container killed on request / signal TERM/HUP received)

• Failures from “bad node” messages and SparkContext shutdown errors

• Entity Attributes & Exclusions Stage
• Spark RDD replica loss

• Container failures, signals received, and cascading abort of job

• Similar issue patterns of RECEIVED SIGNAL TERM and SparkException
Most jobs succeed on retry, but the flaky behavior is inconsistent and currently blocks us from reliably automating Airflow DAGs for nightly runs, as failures are unpredictable and disruptive.
Initial Observations• We use preemptible secondary workers to reduce costs, with autoscaling and Enhanced Flexibility Mode (EFM) enabled

• Many container failures occur on secondary worker nodes

• Patterns indicate possible external container termination, preemption, or infrastructure-level node loss

• The Spark logs repeatedly reference:
• Exit status: -100 / 143

• Container released on a lost node

• No more replicas available for rdd_x_y

• SparkContext shut down unexpectedly

• Signals: RECEIVED SIGNAL TERM or HUP
We do not observe errors in our Airflow orchestration, job configuration, or application logic. Metrics reporting and final-stage writers often abort after a stage-wide executor failure.
This appears to be infrastructure instability, potentially caused by:
• Preemptible VMs being reclaimed mid-job

• YARN container eviction due to memory/cluster pressure

• GCP zone-level issues (e.g., europe-west1-b capacity strain)

• Node loss in Dataproc secondary worker pools impacting data locality and causing RDD replication failures
QCP support ticket:
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

tickets.summary: Handle Dataproc cluster-creation errors
tickets.description:
Dataproc cluster creation still fails intermittently with 503 UNAVAILABLE / COMPUTE_ENGINE when a zone runs out of capacity:
airflow.exceptions.AirflowException: 503 Error Code: UNAVAILABLE, errorSource: COMPUTE_ENGINE,
Error Message: The zone 'projects/ochre-1-dead-dev-1/zones/europe-west1-b'
does not have enough resources available to fulfill the request.Although the QuantexaDataprocClusterCreateOperator now deletes clusters in ERROR state, the DAG still hard-fails when capacity is exhausted and no retry / fallback logic kicks in.
We need a robust strategy that:
• Recognises Dataproc’s resource-exhaustion errors early.

• Retries cluster creation automatically (either after exponential back-off or in an alternative zone/region).

• Ensures the DAG does not proceed until a healthy cluster exists.

• Preserves existing debugging log lines for support.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.testing_required:
• Detection – Operator explicitly detects 503 UNAVAILABLE and other GCE capacity errors during cluster creation.

• Clean-up – Any partially created cluster in ERROR state is deleted before the next attempt.

• Retry strategy –
• Option A: exponential back-off retries in the same zone for a configurable number of attempts.

• Option B: configurable fallback zones/regions (e.g. europe-west1-c, europe-west1-d) used when the primary zone is exhausted.

• Success path – DAG only continues once a cluster reaches a non-ERROR terminal state (RUNNING, UPDATING, etc.).

tickets.summary: Add Dataproc Job Status-Check Stage to ETL DAGs
tickets.description:
Airflow log-retrieval flakiness (*** No logs found in GCS ***, Request URL is missing an 'http://' or 'https://' protocol, 404/Name-Not-Known) is still marking successful Dataproc tasks as failed and breaking downstream stages or cluster-retry logic.

Introduce a new sensor-type stage that, when Airflow logs are missing, polls Dataproc directly (projects.regions.jobs.get) until the job reaches a terminal state. If Dataproc reports SUCCEEDED the DAG continues; if it reports ERROR/CANCELLED the DAG fails with the Dataproc error payload. This creates a robust fallback that bypasses Airflow’s log viewer and prevents false-negative task results.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• A reusable Dataproc job-status sensor (or equivalent stage) exists.

• Every Dataproc-based ETL job in all environments runs through this status check.

• DAG outcomes reflect Dataproc’s true job state, independent of Airflow log availability.

tickets.summary: OS incremental ETL failed in prod (load elastic...again!)
tickets.description:
Incremental load to Elasticsearch failed during OS ETL in production. The job failed with a circuit_breaking_exception indicating data size exceeded memory allocation limits during document indexing. This is a recurring issue impacting Resolver Search loading in production.
Root Cause DescriptionThe Elasticsearch cluster triggered a circuit_breaking_exception due to memory pressure:
[parent] Data too large, data for [<http_request>] would be [~4.7 GB], exceeding limit of [4.5 GB]
Real usage: 4.65–4.7 GB
New bytes reserved: up to ~20 MB
Usages: inflight_requests ~80 MB, others negligibleError stack trace:
org.elasticsearch.hadoop.EsHadoopException:
  org.elasticsearch.hadoop.rest.EsHadoopRemoteException: circuit_breaking_exception: [parent] Data too large

Caused by:
  org.elasticsearch.hadoop.rest.EsHadoopInvalidRequest: [GET] on [] failed; server returned [503|Service Unavailable]

Further root stack:
  com.quantexa.etl.elastic.resolversearch.Elastic8PartitionWriter
  com.quantexa.etl.elastic.resolversearch.utils.ResolverSearchLoaderUtils$.internalLoadDocuments
  ... traced through ResolverSearchLoaderPreloadRunDataReportUtils$
The job was unable to detect the ES version, possibly due to cluster unavailability or missing WAN config (es.nodes.wan.only). These contributed to the failure of the Elastic Writer during the partition write stage.
Slack:

tickets.summary: Investigate and Fix countriesToProcess Filtering Bug in D&B ETL (Prime + Notifications)
tickets.description:
The countriesToProcess filtering logic in the D&B ETL pipeline appears to be broken. Despite passing valid ISO2 country codes, the output is not being subsetted as expected. 

When providing a list of ISO3 codes, no filtering occurred — which is somewhat expected — but the pipeline did not error or return an empty set, making it hard to catch.
• After converting to ISO2 and rerunning, no subsetting occurred either — output was identical to unfiltered runs.

• Further investigation showed that:
• The countriesToProcess filter is applied only to DnbPrime, not DnbPrimeNotifications.

• The feature appears to be incorrectly documented, with the suggestion that both full and incremental notifications are subsetted — this is misleading.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Add country subsetting to slow tests

• Ensure consistent filtering logic is applied to both DnbPrime and DnbPrimeNotifications.

• Fix logic so that filters are respected in both full and incremental modes.

• Add validation to error or warn on invalid country codes (e.g. ISO3 passed instead of ISO2).

tickets.summary: ERQ tooling and analysis
tickets.description:
This task involves executing and analysing the EQ tooling (EQO, EQU, EQX) as part of the baseline entity resolution quality (ERQ) assessment on the BR-processed output from the D&B subset, ICIJ, and Dow Jones datasets.
The purpose is to establish a quality benchmark ahead of Parser 4.3 ER testing. This work will be owned by Data Feeds, with support from DCI for interpretation, and contributions from Spyros (analysis).
Tooling will be run against BR output generated using:
• Parsers 4.2.2

• Quantexa Platform 2.8

• Data Packs v2.3

• Subset Countries: GBR, USA, RUS, JPN, IND, ARE, HKG, BRA, MEX, ESP, PAN
The outcomes will inform config adjustments, parser improvements, or resolver enhancements for the 4.3 testing phase. This ticket covers the initial baseline run, analysis, and any recommendations that emerge.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• EQ tooling (EQO, EQU, EQX) is run on the full BR output for D&B subset, ICIJ, and Dow Jones

• Output is successfully interpreted with DCI support and aligned to known ER quality expectations

• Analysis highlights key patterns (e.g. under/over linking, alias handling, disjoint variants)

• Recommendations are captured and shared with relevant stakeholders for incorporation into Parser 4.3 test scope

tickets.summary: SPIKE Investigate mega docs for potential mitigation
tickets.description:
Consider:
• Dropping traversal / linked record limits

• What traversals have highest count
Dow JonesDuring ETL for DowJones, the extractCompounds stage failed repeatedly with the following error:
org.apache.spark.SparkException: [TASK_WRITE_FAILED] Task failed while writing rows to gs://ochre-1-dead-dev-1-data/processed/etl/iterations/ERQ-er_testing/dowjones/latest/Compounds/DocumentIndexInput.parquet
Caused by: java.lang.IllegalArgumentException: Cannot grow BufferHolder by size 80 because the size after growing exceeds size limitation 2147483632This issue is caused by a specific document (docId = 1053059) that contains an extremely large associates array (~3487 entries). This single array pushes the document size beyond the Spark 2GB BufferHolder threshold, causing the job to crash.
We’ve hit similar issues before in other feeds like Orbis and Grid, and had mitigated via traversal/linked record limits and filtering megadocs. However, this has now re-emerged even in relatively small datasets like DJ, which is concerning.

Minimal cleansed document data model which contains this document: gs://ochre-1-dead-dev-1-data/processed/etl/iterations/ERQ-er_testing/dowjones/latest-debug3/DocumentDataModel/CleansedDocumentDataModel.parquet
[Add examples of Orbis and Grid]
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• The parquet file CleansedDocumentDataModel.parquet is inspected and the offending document's structure is summarized (including array counts and estimated size).

• A mechanism is proposed (and optionally prototyped) for pre-flagging megadocs that risk exceeding Spark's BufferHolder threshold.

• The cause and tradeoffs of using NoOpPartitionSizer in DowJones (vs. safer sizers like in Orbis) are documented.

• A long-term mitigation plan is outlined, including schema, pipeline, or partition sizing changes, and shared with the Data Feeds team for review.

tickets.summary: Investigate Flaky Behaviour in ReprocessingAndDeletesScript
tickets.description:
During a recent incremental run of the project_example_dag_main Airflow pipeline, the AggregatedTransactionReprocessingAndDeletesScript stage failed due to a missing type column in the deletes parquet file. The issue resolved on retry, but given the critical position of this stage near the end of the pipeline, we want to:
• Determine whether the failure was a one-off or indicates flaky or non-deterministic behaviour.

• Understand whether incorrect triggering parameters (e.g. skipped testing stages) contributed to the failure.

• Ensure upstream delete parquet file generation is stable and always conforms to the expected schema.
Failure Details:
• Error: org.apache.spark.sql.AnalysisException: [UNRESOLVED_COLUMN.WITH_SUGGESTION] A column or function parameter with name type cannot be resolved.

• Expected Schema: DocumentId(type: String, value: String)

• Pipeline Run: Airflow DAG project_example_dag_main, run ID: manual__2025-05-12T13:04:40.000+00:00

• GS Path: gs://green-1-accelerators-3-data/airflow-etl/main/transaction/manual--2025-05-12t1757120000/raw/parquet/DeletedTransactionIds.parquet
Thread:
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Root cause of missing type field identified.

• Clear recommendation or implemented fix to ensure this doesn’t happen again.

• Verification of whether the script is flaky or just misconfigured on this specific run.

• Optional: Improvements added to error messaging or validation to make this easier to trace in future runs.

tickets.summary: Improve Robustness of DocumentId Schema Handling in Reprocessing and Delete Scripts
tickets.description:
The ReprocessingAndDeletesScript currently assumes that the input parquet file of delete IDs conforms exactly to the schema of DocumentId(type: String, value: String). However, in some cases, the parquet only includes the value column, which causes the following runtime error when casting directly to Dataset[DocumentId]:
org.apache.spark.sql.AnalysisException: [UNRESOLVED_COLUMN.WITH_SUGGESTION] A column or function parameter with name `type` cannot be resolved.This breaks incremental runs unless the file is manually patched or modified upstream.
A temporary fix may be added to fill in the missing type column during read, but a more robust and permanent solution is needed.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Make the type field in DocumentId optional or adjust the casting logic to handle missing columns more gracefully.

• Alternatively, standardise and enforce the expected schema in the upstream job that generates the delete parquet file.

• Remove the temporary .withColumn("type", ...) patch in ReprocessingAndDeletesScript.

• Ensure backward compatibility with existing files that only contain value.

tickets.summary: Optional Step in Data Packs Pipeline to Build and Upload Internal Feeds JARs
tickets.description:
Currently, the internal data feeds JARs must be manually built and managed locally after publishing the data packs snapshot. This manual step is prone to issues in local environments and adds overhead to the workflow.
To streamline the process, we propose modifying the CI/CD pipeline to:
• Automatically build the internal data feeds JARs immediately after the data packs snapshot is published.

• Upload the generated JARs to the appropriate GCS bucket.
This change will reduce manual intervention, improve reliability across environments, and align better with how the data packs are handled.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• After data packs snapshot is published in Jenkins, CI/CD automatically triggers the internal data feeds JAR build.

• Built JARs are uploaded to the configured GCS bucket.

• A flag or config allows this step to be toggled on/off for flexibility.

• The JARs are versioned or timestamped to avoid overwrite conflicts.

tickets.description:
Implement the subset stage for the ICIJ and OpenSanctions (OS) data sources to maintain consistency and reduce special-case logic in the ETL pipeline.
Although these datasets are already small, applying the subset step (with subset ratio = 1) ensures all sources follow the same ETL structure, avoiding conditional handling downstream.
This will:
• Simplify ETL logic and remove the need for special-case handling in  Airflow DAGs.

• Make the subset DAG architecture consistent across all sources, even those with trivially small subsets.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria: • Verified that subset step returns full dataset (ratio = 1) without error.

tickets.summary: [Incident] Airflow instability due to log flakiness
tickets.description:
Current errors on airflow
airflow2-worker-1.airflow2-worker.airflow2.svc.cluster.local
__* No logs found in GCS; ti=%s [TaskInstance: etl_grid_subset_main.import_raw.import_raw manual__2025-04-17T10:09:46+00:00 [failed]]
__* Could not read served logs: [Errno -2] Name or service not knownairflow2-worker-1.airflow2-worker.airflow2.svc.cluster.local
__* No logs found in GCS; ti=%s [TaskInstance: etl_icij_subset_main.cleanse.dataproc_logger manual__2025-04-17T10:07:43.315267+00:00 [scheduled]]__* No logs found in GCS; ti=%s [TaskInstance: etl_opensanctions_incremental_release_2.7_2.x_1.x.extract_compounds.create_airflow-dpr-release-27-2x-1-medium-n2-opensanc_dataproc_cluster scheduled__2025-03-18T08:00:00+00:00 [failed]]
__* Could not read served logs: Request URL is missing an 'http://' or 'https://' protocol.airflow2-worker-0.airflow2-worker.airflow2.svc.cluster.local
__* No logs found in GCS; ti=%s [TaskInstance: etl_opencorporates_subset_main.import_raw.dataproc_logger manual__2024-12-04T07:53:23+00:00 [failed]]
__* Could not read served logs: Client error '404 NOT FOUND' for url 'http://airflow2-worker-0.airflow2-worker.airflow2.svc.cluster.local:8793/log/dag_id=etl_opencorporates_subset_main/run_id=manual__2024-12-04T07:53:23+00:00/task_id=import_raw.dataproc_logger/attempt=1.log'
For more information check: https://httpstatuses.com/404Latest support ticket:  
 

December 2025 Update - Issue Recurrence & ResolutionLatest Incident (Dec 16, 2025)The missing logs issue resurfaced in ochre-1-dead-dev-1, affecting multiple subset DAG runs with errors:
• No logs found in GCS

• Invalid URL 'http://:8793/log/...' - No host supplied

• Multiple DAGs failing at different stages due to log retrieval issues
Root Cause IdentifiedVentsislav identified the issue as Airflow GitHub Issue #41359 - a known Airflow scheduler race condition:
Technical Details:
• Redis initialization during module import causes timeout in Celery/Kombu

• Default CELERY__OPERATION_TIMEOUT (1 second) is too short

• When Redis takes longer to initialize, scheduler process times out (PID: 7)

• Scheduler marks jobs as zombies and kills them before logs can be uploaded to GCS

• Error manifests as: Process timed out, PID: 7 → Task Timeout Error
Why it happens:
• Scheduler performs inline import of Redis during task distribution

• Redis database initialization can occasionally take >1s

• Timeout causes race condition where worker processes die ungracefully

• Logs never reach GCS because process terminated before upload
Resolution Applied (Dec 16, 2025)Fix: Increased CELERY__OPERATION_TIMEOUT from 1s to 10s via:
• PR: https://github.com/quantexa-internal/google-environments/pull/8031

• Deployed to ochre-1-dead-dev-1 and ochre-1-dead-prod-1

• Also upgraded to latest Airflow version with optimized celery/worker settings
Additional Improvements:
• Added .airflowignore to reduce scheduler overhead (150 files → 3-5 Python files per branch)

• Reduces scheduler scan load which contributed to resource pressure
Limitations• This is a workaround, not a permanent fix - upstream Airflow issue remains open

• Liveness probes don't detect this race condition (don't check Redis health)

• May still occur under extreme load, but frequency should be significantly reduced
Historical Context (April-August 2025)Previous InvestigationsWorker Memory Issues:
• Celery workers running out of memory under high concurrency

• Workers killed before flushing logs to GCS

• 403 Forbidden errors on worker log servers (auth mismatch)

• Race condition with AIRFLOW*LOGGING*DELETE_LOCAL_LOGS=true
Resolutions Applied (August 2025):
• Full environment reset and Airflow upgrade to 2.10.5

• Optimized Celery worker memory settings

• Increased worker resources and reduced concurrency

• Detailed documentation: Airflow Celery Worker Memory Sizing Guide
See also: GCP Airflow & Dataproc Instabilities Summary
Related Issues• Similar errors reported in ochre-1-qsb-dev-1 and saas_banking environments

• Logging API Read Quota exceeded (60 requests/min limit)

• Dataproc cluster creation timeouts correlated with log issues
Next Steps• Monitor stability after December 16 fix

• Consider migration to CeleryKubernetesExecutor (eliminates worker pod issues)

• Track upstream Airflow issue #41359 for permanent fix

tickets.summary: Summarise Significant Changes Between Data Pack Versions
tickets.description:
Analyse and summarise significant differences between v1 and v2 data packs, focusing on changes that may have occurred due to cleansing and entity resolution (ER) pipeline updates. These findings are critical to providing quality assurance to support the argument for deprecating data packs v1 and parsers 3, ensuring no unexpected changes were missed by previous testing.
Objective:
Give senior stakeholders (e.g. Jamie) confidence that dropping support for v1 data packs and parsers 3 won’t introduce regression risks — by clearly showing that any changes are expected, explainable, and not harmful.
Scope:
• Summarise significant differences in ETL behaviour across key data packs:
• Orbis (already analysed)

• D&B

• ICIJ

• DowJones

• Grid

• OpenCorporates

• Focus on the impact of cleansing and ER stages in particular.

• Highlight any large or unexpected shifts in:
• Index sizes

• Document-to-record ratios (doc2rec)

• ETL run durations

• Resource usage (e.g. workers, runtime, memory)

• Cross-reference with this shared workbook:
🔗 Data packs change analysis.xlsx
Explicit Use Case:
These summaries will directly feed into the business case for removing support for:
• Data packs v1

• Parsers 3
…by showing that:
• There are no concerning, untested changes between versions, and

• Performance or index shifts are expected due to environment/hardware changes or are otherwise non-problematic.
Examples (from Orbis):
• 📈 doc2rec is 55% larger → possibly due to more records per document, but no obvious QA concern.

• 📊 Major entity indices (1TB+) are no more than 12% bigger per doc.

• ☎️ Telephone index grew by 22% despite not being changed explicitly in parser logic.

• ⚙️ Cleansing is significantly faster (from 40 workers @ 1h to 4 workers @ 2h, with 36% more docs) → potentially due to:
• Infrastructure change from n1 to n2 machines (≈30% faster).

• Optimised cluster usage.

• Compound may not benefit as much if record count per doc increased.
Goals:
• Determine whether such patterns:
• Are isolated or trends across data packs.

• Indicate any unexplained shifts needing further QA.

• Provide explanations for each observed change — whether due to infra, parser logic, data quality, or ETL tuning.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Bullet-point summaries of changes for each data pack.

• Clear statements on whether the changes are expected, explainable, or concerning.

• Notes on cleansing & ER-specific impacts.

• High-level trends across all data packs, if any.

• Shared with stakeholders in time for v1/p3 deprecation decision.
