tickets.summary: Establish Foundation for Automated ETL Metric Collection
tickets.description:
This epic aims to establish a lightweight, modular, and robust foundation for collecting real resource usage and performance metrics from Spark jobs triggered via Airflow. This effort aims to improve the accuracy and consistency of internal optimisation metrics while reducing reliance on manual, config-based estimation.
This work will start with a narrow MVP scope and expand as we validate our tooling choices and collect feedback from initial usage.
It ties in with ongoing cluster evaluation and tuning efforts and will ensure the data feeds team has reliable and reproducible insights to optimise resource usage, detect anomalies, and improve job stability and cost efficiency.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Clear definition of which metrics are collected and why.

• A working, minimally intrusive prototype collecting metrics post-job.

• No disruption to ETL job performance or Airflow reliability.

• Internal doc explaining usage, limitations, and roadmap.

• Findings from DF-1674, DF-1673, and DF-1534 feed into refinement and expansion.

• Agreement from key stakeholders

comments.author: Usman Kayani

• Implementing spark measure as part of the spark task group generated by AF. Output to durable location.

• Get edit permissions for Grafana and create a simple dashboard to prove we get spark measure stats from durable location

• Remove secondary workers and autoscaling

tickets.summary: Automate Runtime ETL Metric Collection for Airflow-Triggered Spark Jobs
tickets.description:
Business Justification:
Reliable, automated collection of ETL runtime metrics is essential for performance tuning, cost optimisation, and infrastructure stability. This ticket enables data-driven analysis of Spark job behaviour across internal Airflow workflows—reducing reliance on static config estimates and enabling reproducible baselines for scaling, debugging, and delivery planning.
Currently, ETL performance metrics for Spark jobs triggered via Airflow are estimated manually using configuration inputs (e.g. dataProcSettings.json, declared executor counts, cluster sizing, and autoscaling settings). However, these values often diverge from real execution behaviour, due to the effects of Spark dynamic allocation, Dataproc autoscaling, and job-specific data variability.
This ticket will introduce automated metric collection to capture actual runtime metrics, including:
• Spark execution metrics (stage durations, memory usage, executor scaling, I/O, GC, etc.)

• YARN and Dataproc logs (e.g. preemptible loss, cluster creation latency, failures)

• QSS metadata (execution times, parameter settings, runtime stats)

• Cluster and pipeline configuration snapshots for reproducibility
The initial scope focuses on internal data feeds jobs and will extend existing metric extraction scripts with logic to be run after DAG completion, using a downstream Airflow task or external trigger.
This is a foundational enabler for:
• Reducing platform cost via right-sizing and autoscaling insights

• Debugging regressions and tuning performance for Spark-heavy pipelines

• Establishing a performance baseline to support entity resolution testing (e.g. Parser 4.2 → 4.3 comparisons)

• Building toward a consistent internal metric platform across feeds, BR, and analytics workloads
This work supports and is aligned with ongoing infra tuning and profiling epics:
• DF-1674 — Evaluate Secondary Workers and Autoscaling for Dataproc Clusters

• DF-1673 — Reassess Dataproc Cluster Properties and Spark Settings

• DF-1534 — Evaluate and Implement Spark Performance Tuning Tools
The results of this work will inform decisions about Spark tuning, cluster sizing, and Airflow DAG design across the platform.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

parent.summary: Automated Incremental ETL Framework for Scheduled Live ETL refreshes

tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Automate metric collection from Spark jobs triggered via Airflow DAGs, ensuring execution after job completion and without interference to job runtime.

• Integrate Spark History Server or SparkMeasure (as determined by DF-1534) to extract performance metrics per job and per stage.

• Persist extracted metrics in a structured format (e.g. JSON or CSV), partitioned by job ID or DAG run ID for reproducibility.

• Collect and store accompanying cluster configuration (e.g. machine type, executor config, dynamic allocation settings) to support contextual analysis.

• Provide examples of collected metric sets for at least 3 internal data feeds DAGs (e.g. D&B Full, ICIJ, Dowjones) to validate implementation.

• Ensure raw metrics can be reused in future analysis without re-running jobs.

tickets.summary: Excessive Nested Arrays Causing Extract Compounds to Fail
tickets.description:
The extractCompounds stage in the Orbis pipeline is failing due to documents containing excessively large nested arrays — specifically a document (bvdIdNumber: CHCHE106831974) with 23,120 directors. This overwhelms the memory allocation in Spark, leading to buffer overflow errors like Cannot grow BufferHolder by size 24 because the size after growing exceeds size limitation 2147483632.
Our investigation confirmed that these large nested structures are not being filtered or truncated before hitting downstream logic. This is not a scalable pattern and opens the pipeline up to silent breakages when extreme data structures are ingested.
While a manual filter workaround (excluding problematic documents) is currently unblocking the pipeline, this is not a sustainable long-term solution. Ideally, this class of problem should be solved at the ingestion stage (Fusion), where such data structures originate.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• A configurable maximum threshold is introduced for nested arrays in Fusion (e.g. directors, shareholders).

• Documents breaching these thresholds are either:
• Rejected upstream with a validation error, OR

• Truncated to a safe limit before ingestion, with logging for traceability.

• Test using minimal reproducing example: gs://quantexa-ochre-data-lake-orbis/processed/etl/iterations/2.7_2.x_1.x/bvd/problem3/DocumentDataModel/CleansedDocumentDataModel.parquetwhich contains the problematic document CHCHE106831974

Fix this sprint even if its a workaround but long term we want to raise this to fusion

comments.author: Renos Ballas
comments.body:
I agree there needs to be a limit, but we need to service as much of a high limit as possible, or we are losing on ER + one of Q’s selling points (“we can ingest any data”).
Also, rejecting whole rows is not easy to do with clients.

• Should we not look into the Business Analysis side of things and amend the ETL in a way that we carry less information per row?
• For example, looking at DnB data (should apply to Orbis but DnB is what I have on my project right now), 211283215 has ~3k executives [top result]. But if I look at their addresses, most of them are a certain London address. Their job titles have a lot of repetition too “Non-designated LLP member”. So instead of having an array of executives like the below: 
Name
Job Title
Address
Renos Ballas
Non-designated LLP member
GBR London
Ozias Barros
Non-designated LLP member
Non-designated LLP member
we can have:
Name(s)
Job Title
Address
Renos Ballas, Ozias Barros
Non-designated LLP member
GBR London
it is a simplified example, incurs changes to UI viewers, ER fusion config, but you get the idea.

• If we exclude certain elements of the array, some Business Analysis should be done, and not randomly what is above the .count limit. For example, if we were to exclude Shareholders, we should be excluding the ones with minority stakes first.

• There appears to be some repetition. If I call .distinct on the full name, I get about 2800 executives, so I assume we can configure something to assume that if full name + raw address elements are the same then consider them the same? (we need to be able to switch off in case someone does not use name + address elementsf or ER?)

I understand the need for a quick work-around, but longer term I really think these should be Addressed in a best-practice way, so that Datapacks remain to provide the most polished and best-practice ETLs we have.

  keen to know your thoughts

comments.author: Ozias Barros
comments.body:
I agree.
It’s fundamentally a  document modeling design improvement. 
We should aim to reduce Data Size per Row
• Avoid or limit large arrays. Instead of aggregating massive amounts of data into a single row, consider splitting it into multiple smaller rows or grouping differently. Grouping differently will also reduce massively the size of the document.

comments.author: Ozias Barros
comments.body: Hey  ,   Could you share a link to the ticket where the proper fix for this tactical solution is being tracked?

tickets.summary: Airflow Orbis CCC stage failure
tickets.description:
Airflow DAG failure caused by an incompatible change in config/dataProcSettings.json where disk sizes were updated from G to GB. This change broke parsing in Airflow, leading to a ValueError in dd_creator.py.
Issue Details:
• DAG Name: etl_dag.py

• Failure Traceback: 
File "/usr/local/airflow/airflow-bucket/dags/main/modules/dynamic_dag_helpers/dd_creator.py", line 470, in <listcomp>
    self.get_spark_task_group(
File "/usr/local/airflow/airflow-bucket/dags/main/modules/dynamic_dag_helpers/dd_creator.py", line 207, in get_spark_task_group
    "master_boot_disk_size": int(master_boot_disk_size.replace("G", "")),
                                ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: '250B'

• Root Cause:
• The change in config/dataProcSettings.json from 250G to 250GB led to incorrect string parsing in Airflow.

• The Airflow script expects G, while the Dataproc script expects GB.
Related Logs & Errors:
• Andrew Jensen reported an issue in CCC for Orbis Parquet: 
googleapiclient.errors.HttpError: <HttpError 400 when requesting ... returned "Invalid value at 'cluster.config.master_config.disk_config.boot_disk_size_gb' (TYPE_INT32), "250G"

• Config Change Diff: 
- "GOOGLE_HADOOP_MASTER_BOOT_DISK_SIZE": "250G"
+ "GOOGLE_HADOOP_MASTER_BOOT_DISK_SIZE": "250GB"
Resolution Steps:
• Ensure both Airflow and the scripts handle disk size consistently.

• Modify Airflow’s dd_creator.py to properly handle GB values: 
"master_boot_disk_size": int(master_boot_disk_size.replace("GB", "").replace("G", "")),

• Validate that Dataproc still correctly interprets GB values.

• Test both Airflow DAG execution and CCC ETL pipeline for successful runs.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

tickets.summary: Orbis Parquet ETL runtime issues
tickets.description:
A new problem has emerged when running Orbis Parquet on create case class. The error occurs in the processTimestampDate function within ParquetIngestUtils when handling timestamp conversion.
Error Details:Text '2024/02' could not be parsed, unparsed text found at index 0
	at java.base/java.time.format.DateTimeFormatter.parseResolved0(DateTimeFormatter.java:2055)
	at java.base/java.time.format.DateTimeFormatter.parse(DateTimeFormatter.java:1880)
	at com.quantexa.orbis.ingest.utils.ParquetIngestUtils$.processTimestampDate(ParquetIngestUtils.scala:459)
	at com.quantexa.orbis.ingest.utils.ParquetIngestUtils$.transformShareholderRawParquetToOrbisShareholder(ParquetIngestUtils.scala:389)
	at com.quantexa.orbis.ingest.OrbisSourceIngest.$anonfun$preprocessShareholdersParquet$4(OrbisSourceIngest.scala:705)
	at com.quantexa.orbis.ingest.OrbisSourceIngest.$anonfun$aggregateDataset$1(OrbisSourceIngest.scala:442)
Root Cause Hypothesis:• The error suggests that the input date format is yyyy/MM (e.g., "2024/02") instead of the expected formats in processTimestampDate: 
def processTimestampDate(inputDate: Option[String]): Option[String] = {
  val datetimeFormat = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("[yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss]" + "[yyyy-MM-dd]")
  val dateFormat = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("yyyy-MM-dd")
  inputDate match {
    case Some(date) => Option(dateFormat.format(datetimeFormat.parse(date)))
    case _          => inputDate
  }
}

• The function expects either "yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss" or "yyyy-MM-dd", but the input does not conform.

• This suggests a new date format (yyyy/MM) appearing in CCC data that is not handled by our formatter.
Next Steps / Investigation Plan:• Review the raw data to confirm the input format.

• Run Orbis parquet E2E on dev using scripts to replicate the issue.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
✅ The processTimestampDate function correctly handles unexpected date formats without throwing exceptions. Ensure compatibility with all valid input formats, including yyyy/MM.
✅ Confirm fixes in dev before deploying to prod (too many failures in prod currently).

tickets.summary: Orbis Parquet Validation investigation
tickets.description:
The Orbis Parquet ETL job is failing at the ValidateRawData stage due to a schema mismatch in the more_evaluation_year_ field. The validation expects a 4-digit year string, but the parquet files contain timestamps (yyyy-01-01 00:00:00).
Issue Details:
• The validation rule enforces a max length of 4 for more_evaluation_year_, causing failures for 261,490,904 rows where the field contains a full timestamp instead.

• The CSV version of the data correctly stores this field as an integer year, whereas the Parquet version converts it into a timestamp.

• Investigation confirmed that all timestamps follow the pattern xx-01-01 00:00:00.

• A quick fix is needed to extract the year from the timestamp before validation, similar to how processTimestampDate handles other date conversions.
Thread:
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• The ETL pipeline extracts only the year from more_evaluation_year_ timestamps before validation.

• The ETL job completes without failure at ValidateRawData.

tickets.summary: Airflow DAG Trigger Failures Silently Ignored in Nightly Jenkins Pipeline
tickets.description:
The nightly Jenkins pipeline for promoting and triggering Airflow DAGs intermittently fails to trigger DAGs successfully due to timing issues related to DAG file deletion and re-upload during the promotion stage. This results in Airflow temporarily being unable to locate DAG files, causing trigger commands to fail. Additionally, Airflow sometimes shows has_import_errors = True, indicating potential DAG parsing issues after promotion. The Jenkins pipeline does not detect these failures because the build() function is configured with wait: false, leading to false-positive success notifications. This affects workflows like QSB and others relying on the same promotion/triggering mechanism.
On Jenkins, the error is given like this:
command terminated with exit code 1However the pipeline does write to an err.log file which HAT may have access to: e.g

kubectl exec -n airflow2 airflow2-scheduler-69ddb6ddbc-kc9gm -- bash -c airflow dags trigger etl_opensanctions_subset_main -e 2025-02-06T20:08:00 &> err.log
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Jenkins pipeline should fail if any DAG trigger attempt fails, including cases where has_import_errors = True.

• Slack notifications in data-feeds-cicd should accurately reflect DAG trigger status (success or failure).

• Implement retry logic or waiting mechanisms to handle DAG file availability delays after promotion.

tickets.summary: Evaluate Secondary Workers and Autoscaling for Dataproc Clusters
tickets.description:
To improve Dataproc cluster stability, we need to evaluate the role of secondary workers and the effectiveness of autoscaling in handling workload fluctuations, particularly in shuffle-heavy jobs. This evaluation will help determine the most efficient cluster configuration, balancing performance, cost, and stability.
Scope:• Evaluate Secondary Workers
• Determine their impact on shuffle performance, including data locality, network usage, and execution efficiency.

• Identify whether they contribute to shuffle inefficiencies, increased job duration, or job failures.

• Consider removing or optimising their configuration if secondary workers introduce unnecessary overhead.

• Assess Autoscaling
• Analyse whether autoscaling improves or hinders performance by evaluating job execution times, resource utilisation, and stability.

• Identify cases where static configurations might provide better reliability over dynamically adjusting resources.

• Investigate whether frequent autoscaling events cause delays, increased JVM GC time, or inefficient CPU allocation.
To complete the assessment we can use:
• Spark Measure – To capture fine-grained execution metrics such as shuffle delays, executor CPU utilisation, and memory usage.

• Spark UI & Event Logs – This is used to analyse stage-level performance, shuffle dependencies, and identify resource bottlenecks.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

parent.summary: Establish Foundation for Automated ETL Metric Collection

tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Assess the impact of secondary workers on shuffle-heavy jobs.

• Determine if autoscaling configurations need adjustment or if a static approach is preferable.

• Document findings and recommendations for cluster setup.

issue_links.summary: Automate Runtime ETL Metric Collection for Airflow-Triggered Spark Jobs

comments.author: Usman Kayani
comments.body:
Evaluating Secondary Workers📌 Goal: Determine whether secondary workers contribute to shuffle-related inefficiencies.
Steps:
• Compare shuffle metrics between jobs executed with and without secondary workers:
• Look at shuffleTotalBytesRead, shuffleRecordsRead, shuffleBytesWritten

• If secondary workers cause high shuffle latency (shuffleFetchWaitTime), they might negatively impact performance.

• If shuffleRemoteBytesRead is disproportionately high, it may indicate excessive remote disk I/O, which secondary workers may not be handling efficiently.

• Assess executor runtimes and CPU efficiency:
• Compare executorRunTime and executorCpuTime between runs with secondary workers enabled vs disabled.

• If secondary workers are not contributing much, executorCpuTime will remain the same or increase (indicating unnecessary task splitting).

• Look for memory and spill-related issues:
• If secondary workers lead to higher memoryBytesSpilled or diskBytesSpilled, it suggests inefficiency in shuffle management.
Outcome: If secondary workers cause unnecessary shuffle or inefficiencies, consider removing them for shuffle-heavy jobs.

comments.author: Usman Kayani
comments.body:
Assessing Autoscaling📌 Goal: Determine whether autoscaling provides benefits or if a static configuration is better.
Steps:
• Analyse job execution times across different cluster sizes:
• If autoscaled clusters show inconsistent stage duration (stageDuration) or high executor wait times, it could indicate that frequent node additions/removals are causing disruptions.

• Compare with static configurations to check if job completion is more predictable.

• Check CPU utilisation:
• If executorCpuTime is not scaling proportionally with increasing cluster size, it suggests autoscaling might be adding excessive nodes without actual performance gain.

• Look for signs of under-utilisation:
• If autoscaling increases the cluster size but peakExecutionMemory and executorRunTime remain unchanged, it indicates that additional resources are not being effectively used.

• Compare GC times (jvmGCTime):
• If autoscaling leads to higher GC times, it may suggest frequent memory allocation/deallocation issues due to dynamic scaling.
Outcome: If autoscaling is found to introduce instability or unnecessary resource allocation, a static cluster configuration may be more stable.

tickets.summary: Reassess Dataproc Cluster Properties and Spark Settings for Stability
tickets.description:
To enhance cluster performance, we need to optimize Dataproc cluster settings based on workload types.
Scope:• Memory-Intensive Jobs: Increase executor memory and overhead to reduce garbage collection.

• CPU-Intensive Jobs: Adjust executor cores for better parallelism.

• Mixed Workloads: Balance memory and CPU to maintain stability.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

tickets.summary: Improve Cluster Create Operator Error Handling
tickets.description:
Currently, the cluster create operator incorrectly reports success even if the cluster enters an error state immediately. This behaviour may be caused by the existing retry mechanism (delete_on_error=True), which silently fails and continues retrying.
To improve reliability, we need to modify the operator to differentiate between error types:
• The operator should continue retrying if the error is due to resource constraints.

• The operator should immediately fail the job if the error is a non-recoverable failure.
The delete cluster operator should delete the cluster that enters an error state
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Modify the cluster create operator to properly detect and handle immediate error states.

• Implement logic to differentiate between resource-related errors (which should trigger retries) and other failures (which should fail the job).

• Ensure that non-recoverable errors cause the job to fail and do not get silently retried.

• Validate that the correct status is reported in logs and monitoring tools.

tickets.summary: Develop a tool for efficient Spark log retrieval after cluster termination
tickets.description:
Currently, retrieving Spark logs for application components (executors, nodes, etc.) after a cluster has been spun down is challenging due to limitations in the existing tools. Logs are either stored in YARN while the cluster is active or saved as large, unstructured JSON files in the sink, which is not user-friendly.
The goal of this task is to explore and develop a solution that can efficiently retrieve and organise Spark application logs for easier debugging and analysis. This may include:
• Accessing YARN logs using the web API or GCP methods while the cluster is active.

• Retrieving logs stored in buckets or through Log Explorer after cluster termination.

• Developing a new, user-friendly tool or enhancing existing scripts (e.g., dataprocLogging.sh) for log retrieval.

• Speak to HAT about improving the logging outputs
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• A clear understanding of all possible log retrieval options (YARN API, Log Explorer, bucket retrieval).

• A functional tool or script that retrieves Spark application logs and separates them by components (executors, nodes, etc.).

• Documentation on how to use the tool/script, including its limitations.

• Compatibility with existing GCP infrastructure and security standards (e.g., handling access tokens securely).

Additionally, we want to enforce better deployment controls in Ochre Prod:

These changes require coordination with HAT to align on best practices and ensure smooth implementation.

tickets.summary: Implement Validation for Null Values in Data Packs
tickets.description:
A recent bug (DF-1549) in Dow Jones highlighted the need for robust null value validation. While the issue has been resolved, this ticket ensures a standardised validation framework across all data sources.

All data sources will follow a shared validation framework (AbstractDataValidation), integrating with DatasourceValidator to enforce validation rules. Validation will occur at file, dataset, and row levels, triggering exceptions on failure to maintain data integrity. We want to introduce null checks in the performDatasetValidation function in the abstract class and introduce particular null validation thresholds for each data source.
Scope of Work:
• Analysis: Identify gaps in existing validation logic, define nullability requirements, and establish thresholds for critical fields (check real data).

• Implementation: Enhance validation logic in scripts, log null value occurrences, null validation threshold, and improve error messaging.

• Testing: Develop integration tests covering edge cases and large null volumes.

• Documentation: Update data packs documentation with new validation rules.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Standardised null validation across all data sources.

• Critical fields cannot contain nulls without appropriate errors/warnings.

• Comprehensive test coverage ensures validation robustness.

• Documentation updates are completed.

tickets.summary: Consolidate and ReprocessAndAggregate scripts require document ids to delete in two diferent formats.
tickets.description:
The fusion generated Consolidate and Reprocess scripts take a parquet file containing a sequence of document ids to delete from data. 
In the consolidate script the expected schema for this file is one column corresponding the the documentId field name which is defined in config by user as the idField. 
In the ReprocessAndDelete script the expected schema is (`type` , value). 
Both scripts should use the same schema for this parquet file as currently users would need to have one file for each script.
tickets.creator: Ciaran McFadden
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
Both stages run successfully using same parquet file.
Wrap parquet reader in logic to handle both schemas for backward compatibility

tickets.summary: Incremental ETL scripts should not require doc IDs to delete
tickets.description:
The fusion generated Consolidate and ReprocessAndDelete scripts require users to pass in a list of document IDs to delete from their historic ETL runs. If there are no document ids to delete users are required to pass in an empty parquet file instead to these scripts. 
This is a clunky workflow and instead users should be able to configure whether or not they have documents to delete prior to running scripts as often projects don’t actually have deletes to include in their incremental runs.
tickets.creator: Ciaran McFadden
tickets.acceptance_criteria: Consolidate and ReprocessAndDelete scripts run successfully with and without delete files passed in through config.

tickets.summary: Airflow DAGs and Stages Incorrectly Report as Failed
tickets.description:
We are encountering an issue where Airflow is incorrectly reporting certain DAGs and stages as failed despite the corresponding jobs completing successfully. This behavior has been observed on multiple occasions, including:
• Batch Resolver DAG: The post_fusion stage was reported as failed even though the batch_resolver stage was successful. (reported by Adam here)

• Graph Scripting DAG: The DAG was marked as failed, but all individual jobs, including the graph scripting stage, completed successfully. (reported by Usman here)

• CTR Submission Stage: The CTR output stage was marked as failed, but logs and Spark job statuses indicate successful completion. (reported by Usman here)
This behavior produces false negatives in monitoring and undermines pipeline reliability, potentially affecting downstream processes and alerting accuracy.
Key Issues Identified:
• Child DAG → Parent DAG: Success in the child DAG is being misreported as a failure in the parent DAG. High Priority - TriggerDAGOperator is sometimes incorrectly failing and its causing PR checks to fail (sometimes even nightly)

• Dataproc → Airflow: Tasks like Spark jobs succeeding on Dataproc are being misreported as failed in Airflow.
Notes:
• The operator logs end without showing any errors.

• Logs show 404 errors when attempting to fetch served logs, indicating potential misconfiguration or transient issues in the logging service.

• Scheduler and worker logs may provide insights and can be accessed via the GCP Log Explorer or kubectl.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

parent.summary: Developer and operations convenience and documentation

tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Investigate the underlying issue causing the false negatives in DAG and stage statuses; work with HAT to find a resolution.

• Ensure accurate reporting in the Airflow UI and logs.

tickets.summary: Upgrade ElasticSearch Cluster in Solar Pre-Prod
tickets.description:
The current ElasticSearch cluster (solar-1-qsb-disc-1) in the Solar pre-production environment has fewer nodes than expected for its workload. This has resulted in load issues, including rejected operations (es_rejected_execution_exception) and network failures during high-volume data loads.
The issue is particularly apparent during specific job runs, such as txn-generic-load_elastic and txn-agg-txn-load-elastic, where reducing the number of Spark executors temporarily alleviates the problem. However, this workaround constrains the pipeline’s performance and scalability.
To address these challenges, the following steps are required:
• Document processes to retrieve ElasticSearch logs using GCP tools such as kubectl logs and gcloud logging read.

• Work with Greg and the QSB team to finalize the size and capacity requirements for the upgraded cluster based on the load metrics and shard distribution. This may include increasing the size or number of nodes in the cluster to handle larger workloads.

• Simulate high-volume data loads to validate cluster behavior under stress and determine scaling needs.

• Optimize configurations for Spark and Dataproc to prevent excessive pressure on ElasticSearch endpoints during job execution.

Thread:  
CPT ticket for tracking:
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

parent.summary: Developer and operations convenience and documentation

tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Cluster Sizing and Requirements:
• Work with Greg and other stakeholders to determine the appropriate node count and storage size for the upgraded cluster.

• Provide a rationale for the sizing decision, supported by metrics such as shard counts, index sizes, and anticipated growth.

• Simulated Load Testing:
• Conduct load tests simulating high-volume data scenarios to measure performance (e.g., request latency, shard rebalancing, rejected requests).

• Confirm smooth cluster operation without es_rejected_execution_exception or similar errors.

• Configuration Optimization:
• Review and update Spark and Dataproc configurations to align with ElasticSearch capabilities.

• Ensure Spark clusters are optimized for parallel execution without overwhelming ElasticSearch.

• Documentation and Stakeholder Review:
• Document the findings from sizing analysis, load testing, and configuration updates on Confluence.

• Share results with QSB and HAT teams for review and approval.

tickets.summary: Adjust Parent DAG Flow to Retrigger Child DAGs from Failed Stages Only
tickets.description:
Currently, the parent DAGs trigger child DAGs for various tasks, but tracking and managing execution lineage is challenging when failures occur.
If a stage in a child DAG fails, the current process requires:
• Manually clearing the failed task in the child DAG.

• Marking the parent DAG as successful.
This approach is cumbersome and prone to errors. To streamline the process, the parent DAG should be improved to automatically retrigger the child DAG from the exact failed stage using the same run_id, without restarting the entire child DAG.
This enhancement will simplify failure handling, improve lineage tracking, and save time during troubleshooting.
Remove all grand children by flattening, reduce the polling time to 15 s (currently a min)
Look into existing airflow features first
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

parent.summary: Developer and operations convenience and documentation

tickets.acceptance_criteria:
Failure Detection and Retrying:
Update the parent DAG to detect failed stages in the child DAG and ensure retriggering starts from the failed stage using the same run_id.
Child DAG Modifications:
Modify child DAGs for resuming at specific failed stages instead of full restarts.
Lineage Tracking:
Ensure consistent lineage tracking and clear logging of the retrigger stage.
Testing and Validation:
Test various failure scenarios to confirm the child DAG resumes correctly and verify that the run_id is retained during retriggers.

tickets.summary: Update Solar Configuration to Use Flowable API URL and Key from Airflow Connections
tickets.description:
Currently, the solar.conf file relies on hardcoded values for the Flowable API URL and API key. This setup lacks flexibility and violates best practices for secure and scalable configuration management.
To address this, we need to update the solar.conf so that it dynamically retrieves the Flowable API URL and API key from environment variables. These environment variables will be set by Airflow connections, using the new connection ID provided by Greg.
This improvement ensures that configuration changes can be managed centrally in Airflow and removes sensitive information from the codebase.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

parent.summary: Implement feature branch promotion to Solar using Jenkins

tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Configuration Update:
• Update solar.conf to fetch the Flowable API URL and API key from environment variables.

• Airflow Connection Integration:
• Ensure that the required environment variables are set using the new Airflow connection ID.

• Validate that the Airflow connection correctly supplies the API URL and key.

• Testing and Validation:
• Test the updated configuration to confirm that the Flowable integration works as expected.

• Validate the changes in both staging and production environments.

tickets.summary: Airflow Stage Logs Missing: 404 Error When Accessing Task Logs from GCS
tickets.description:
In our Airflow stages, we are encountering an issue where logs for certain tasks are not found in GCS. When attempting to access these logs, the following error is returned:  
*** No logs found in GCS; ti=%s <TaskInstance: fulton_ctr_detection_fulton_13_months.aggregate-customer-transactions.aggregate-customer-transactions manual__2024-12-11T22:23:45.619618+00:00 [failed]>  
*** Could not read served logs: Client error '404 NOT FOUND' for url 'http://airflow2-worker-0.airflow2-worker.airflow2.svc.cluster.local:8793/log/dag_id=fulton_ctr_detection_fulton_13_months/run_id=manual__2024-12-11T22:23:45.619618+00:00/task_id=aggregate-customer-transactions.aggregate-customer-transactions/attempt=1.log'This issue occurs intermittently and prevents access to logs for debugging failed tasks, impacting our ability to resolve errors promptly.  
The problem seems related to tasks not correctly uploading logs to GCS, or an issue with the Airflow worker serving the logs from the local file system when logs are not yet available in GCS.

Slack thread:
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

parent.summary: Developer and operations convenience and documentation

tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Logs Uploaded to GCS: All task logs must be reliably uploaded to GCS at the end of task execution, regardless of the task state (success or failure).  

• 404 Error Mitigation: Ensure that Airflow handles missing logs gracefully, either by providing a more actionable error message or retrying log access automatically.  

• Monitoring and Alerts: Add monitoring and alerts for log upload failures to detect and resolve the issue promptly in the future.

tickets.summary: Recurring Issue with Delta-Spark Jar Download Failure on DataProc
tickets.description:
A recurring issue is preventing DataProc from downloading the delta-spark jar (version 3.0.0), causing disruptions to workflows that depend on this package. This error reappears intermittently, affecting the stability and functionality of dependent stages.
Error Details:
• java.lang.RuntimeException: [download failed: io.delta#delta-spark_2.12;3.0.0!delta-spark_2.12.jar]

• java.lang.RuntimeException: [unresolved dependency: io.delta#delta-spark_2.12;3.0.0: not found]
Related Context:
This issue mirrors a previous one in April 2024 related to the DeltaCatalog, where Airflow workflows had compatibility issues with Delta Lake. As part of troubleshooting at that time, Domonkos Kakas added io.delta:delta-spark_2.12:3.0.0 directly to the spark-submit command within quantexaDataprocOperators.py to align with Spark 3.5. Although this workaround resolved the issue for testing, the final fix was to integrate the dependency into the build.gradle file for better version control and consistent environment management.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

parent.summary: Developer and operations convenience and documentation

tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Resolution of Dependency Download Issue: The delta-spark jar (version 3.0.0) should be consistently downloadable without errors, ensuring uninterrupted execution of workflows.

• Version Control Integrity: Ensure that the build.gradle file is correctly configured with the necessary dependencies, preventing the need for temporary workarounds.

tickets.summary: Tasks Stall When Scheduled Before Preceding Task
tickets.description: We are experiencing a bug in Airflow where tasks that are scheduled before their immediate predecessor remain in the "scheduled" state and do not start running. These tasks only begin execution once the task they depend on has started or completed. This behavior is blocking parallel processing of stages in workflows with complex dependencies, leading to delays in task execution and inefficient resource usage.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria: Tasks should be able to run as scheduled, independent of the task immediately preceding them, without waiting for it to run or complete. They should not remain in the "scheduled" state if they have no further dependencies or blockers and any that can run concurrently should do so without delays.

tickets.summary: Cluster Not Deleted on Stage Failure When Overridden [0]
tickets.description:
When overriding the cluster for a specific stage in the ETL pipeline, the cluster is not deleted if the stage fails. The main delete process only targets the medium cluster and does not account for clusters overridden per stage. While the current setup ensures cluster deletion upon successful stage execution (via the all_done trigger rule), no such mechanism exists for handling failures.
This behaviour leads to orphaned clusters, resulting in unnecessary resource consumption and potential cost implications.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

parent.summary: Deploy Fulton DAG on QSB Solar Environment

tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Ensure that clusters overridden for specific stages are deleted regardless of the task's success or failure.

• Add a delete task within the affected task group that triggers in both success and failure scenarios.

• Validate the solution by testing pipeline execution with overridden clusters in both successful and failing stage conditions.

• Confirm no unintended impact on clusters used for other stages in the pipeline.

tickets.summary: Fix DJ dataset validation bug
tickets.description:
Problem code was introduced with intermediary QSS
preprocessedDataset.filter($"personEntityId".isNotNull)
This always returns an empty ds meaning its always validated

“in Spark, comparisons with null using standard equality (==, !=, .equal, or .notEqual) behave in a special way due to the concept of three-valued logic. Comparisons involving null always result in null (not true or false), and since null is neither true nor false, no rows satisfy the filter, causing the dataset to appear empty.
The correct way to check for null values in Spark is to use the .isNotNull method” 
fix is already in main, however we shouldnt be filtering.
remove filtering - it should have already been fixed (I think we just forgot to move it out of data validation) 
To remove you’ll have to adjust abstract validation and remove the option to filter, only DJ is using this.
remove it from other data pack versions.
if it errors then we need to back port the fix
tickets.creator: Cathal Holloway
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• preprocessedDataset.filter($"personEntityId".isNotNull) removed from all data pack versions (and make sure the bug fix is in place for these data pack versions…. if not we will need to back port the fix)

• slowtests passing

• note of bugfix in docs

tickets.summary: Evaluate and Implement Spark Performance Tuning Tools for ETL Optimisation
tickets.description:
As part of our ongoing efforts to improve the efficiency of our ETL jobs on GCP Dataproc with Apache Spark, this task will involve researching, evaluating, and potentially implementing one or more performance-tuning tools. The goal is to optimise our resource usage, reduce job execution time, and lower overall costs associated with computing resources.
Tasks:
• Research available Spark performance tools: Evaluate the pros, cons, and use cases of the following tools:
• sparkMeasure: Collects detailed Spark job metrics like memory usage, shuffle times, and garbage collection to help with detailed performance analysis. 

• Sparklint: A real-time monitoring tool to track task execution and identify skew and bottlenecks.

• Dr. Elephant: Provides automated tuning recommendations by analysing Spark job metrics.

• SparkLens: Offers insights into task parallelism, enabling the identification of underutilised resources.

• Sparkoscope: A profiling tool that collects performance data to help debug and optimise Spark jobs.
• Assess their compatibility with our stack:
• Focus on tools that align with our current architecture (Scala-based jobs, Dataproc, Spark on GCP).

• Ensure the chosen tools integrate smoothly into our existing workflows.
• Test selected tools
Implement the most promising tools in a test environment. Measure the performance gains, focusing on resource usage, job completion time, and cost efficiency.
• Make recommendations for production:
• Based on testing results, recommend implementing one or more tools in our production pipeline.

• Include any additional configuration changes required for optimisation.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

parent.summary: Establish Foundation for Automated ETL Metric Collection

tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• A report summarising the research, pros, cons, and suitability of each tool. A working implementation of the selected tool(s) in the test environment, with performance benchmarking. 

• A recommendation for moving forward with the optimal tool(s) in production.

tickets.description:
To support the entire QSB teams, including Selenium testing, we must deploy a QSB environment in green-1-rnd-dev-2. This deployment requires dummy indices to be used, as real data from ochre cannot be accessed due to PII concerns. The dummy indices will allow basic authentication with shared users, ensuring secure testing without compromising data security.

• If no suitable indices are available, generate dummy indices using the datagen DAG or other scripts.

• Work with relevant team members to copy or generate OS and OC data indices in green-1-rnd-dev-2.

• Confirm whether Airflow needs additional scripts to automate the data-loading process.

• Verify the compatibility of the generated indices with the deployment environment to avoid any future issues.

This request is from .

tickets.summary: NullPointerException During Airflow DAG Promotion to Production
tickets.description: When promoting DAGs to the production Airflow environment, the pipeline fails due to a NullPointerException. The failure is not caused by the missing vault key as initially suspected. The error stems from the processConfigFile function in the pipeline script, where the replaceContentInFile method throws the exception. This issue impacts the promotion of DAGs to production, though the same process works for the development environment.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

• Ensure that the replaceContentInFile method does not throw a null pointer exception and handles the replacements correctly.

• Successfully promoted DAGs to production without pipeline failure.

• Provide documentation or notes if any changes are required to configuration or code to fix the issue.

tickets.summary: Investigate OC Incremental Issues with Load Elastic - Performance Degradation
tickets.description:
• The load elastic stage of the OpenCorporates ETL process is experiencing significant performance degradation during the incremental run for June 2024.

• Performance Issue:
• The stage took over 26 hours to complete, compared to 12 hours in the previous run (even when the indices were corrupted).

• This extended runtime is concerning, especially since the job involves only incremental data processing.

• Context:
• The load elastic stage has not been modified recently, yet the performance has worsened. Given that the stage took 12 hours previously, the current 26-hour runtime indicates a possible underlying issue that needs to be addressed.
This may require updating the spark settings for this stage, modifying the number of executors and CPUs.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Investigate the root cause of the performance degradation in the load elastic stage.

• Optimize the stage to reduce the runtime to an acceptable level (ideally less than 6 hours).

• Document any changes made and validate the performance improvement by rerunning the incremental job.

tickets.summary: Fix Airflow Success Marking for Cancelled Jobs
tickets.description:
• We've identified a critical issue where Airflow is incorrectly marking cancelled jobs as successful on the next retry. This problem was observed during the consolidate compounds stage for the June 2024 incremental run of the OpenCorporates ETL process.

• Error Details:
• Example log: [2024-08-11, 08:59:58 UTC] {quantexa_dataproc_operators.py:226} INFO - Match found with labels task_id consolidate-compoundsconsolidate-compounds and run_id scheduled--2024-05-03t1200000000. Current job state: CANCELLED

• Despite the job being cancelled, Airflow marked the task as SUCCESS on the next retry.

• Context:
• This issue has led to subsequent stages, such as load elastic, running on incomplete or incorrect data. It is crucial to address this to prevent inaccurate data processing in the pipeline.

• Approach:
• Externally cancel a gcloud job and see how airflow reacts in retries.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

tickets.summary: Investigate OC Compounds Incremental Error: Timeout and Job Cancellations [2]
tickets.description:
• During the consolidate compounds stage of the OpenCorporates ETL for the first increment (June 2024), we are encountering timeout and job cancellation issues.

• Timeout Error:
• The job fails due to a timeout error: airflow.exceptions.AirflowTaskTimeout: Timeout, PID: 27795. This timeout triggers a cancellation of the Dataproc job, resulting in a DriverExitStatus with code 143, which suggests possible memory pressure.

• Context:
• This stage has consistently failed during the incremental run for June 2024. The job ran successfully in full mode during the May 2024 run but has not completed successfully in incremental mode.

• The timeout seems to be related to the duration of the job, which has been running for up to 7 hours locally. We may need to investigate resource allocation, timeout settings, or other potential bottlenecks in the ETL pipeline.

• Approach
• Run with subset to identify issues and gauge time of full run
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Identify the root cause of the timeout and job cancellation issues.

• Propose and implement a solution to prevent these errors from recurring.

• Test the solution to ensure that the consolidate compounds stage can run successfully without timeouts or cancellations.

comments.author: Lily Goodyer
comments.body:
Studying the repo code and researching online I have noticed that the default driver_cores is set to 1.
Timeouts (Code 143): If the driver is too slow to keep up with the task orchestration, especially in larger jobs, it could time out, leading to the DriverExitStatus with code 143. This can result from the operating system or resource manager (YARN) terminating the driver due to it becoming unresponsive or not meeting resource expectations.

comments.author: Lily Goodyer
comments.body:
spark.driver.maxResultSize : 
• Purpose: The spark.driver.maxResultSize configuration sets a limit on the maximum size of serialized results that can be sent back to the Spark driver. By default, this is set to 1 GB. If the result exceeds this size, Spark will throw an exception.

• Use Case: If your Spark job produces large result sets that exceed this limit, it can lead to memory issues or disk space issues if results are serialized and stored temporarily before being sent to the driver.
Currently set to 10g

comments.author: Lily Goodyer
comments.body:
Result of increasing worker disk size to 100g:
Success!
Took 52.29 min to run
In addition, looking at the YARN UI indicates that a lot of tasks were being killed.
This correlates with YARN pending memory often reaching ~1-1.5Tbytes + most of the YARN memory being allocated, with only a small amount available, indicating the cluster is under heavy load and is likely running into resource constraints.

comments.author: Lily Goodyer
comments.body:
Delta lake MERGE operation is the most costly/time consuming stage to run.
In addition, every time this stage is run on identical data, new files are created. This could lead to this stage getting subsequently longer and longer, since:
Increased File Count: As new files accumulate due to each MERGE operation, Spark has to scan through a larger number of files for each operation. 
NB: The MERGE operation should only use the files for the current delta version! 
Not sure why it repeatedly adds new files per run even when the data is identical (down to the data_id) → last 3 runs are all on latest-subset → could be partitioning issues?

comments.author: Lily Goodyer
comments.body:
Running a larger subset in incremental to assess the assumption of linear scaling in resource requirements with original data size:
subset data location: gs://ochre-1-dead-dev-1-data/raw/opencorporates/default/snapshot/YYYY-MM/2024-07-subset/uncompressed - 13.4 GiB
Original: gs://quantexa-ochre-data-lake-opencorporates/raw/default/snapshot/YYYY-MM/2024-07/uncompressed - 144.8 GiB
This subset is ~9.5 % the original full data. Consequently, we should see requirements scale up in linear proportion with this % increase in size vs the original 2% subset size, if my initial assumption is correct.

RESULTS:
Maintaining 100G disk size in line with the original successful run, but using 2005 partitions. Will do another run to see if 10000 partitions has an impact on the larger subset in a way it did not appear to for the smaller subset.
Trial with small-n2 and large-n2:
FAILED!
Trying to run on small-n2 and large-n2 with worker disk space set to 100g led to the same issue, which was nodes becoming unhealthy, and this leading to several unsuccessful re-attempts of the consolidate compound stages. The health report for these unhealthy nodes all read “…used space above 90% threshold…”, indicating that again disk space became an issue. This then appears to lead to a SIGNAL TERM which causes the attempt to shutdown and fail.:

729335890  MB seconds

Memory usage went up by 5.9%, which is greater than the linear increase in subset size (4.75%) but within the same magnitude. The average vcore usage went up by 13.5%, a magnitude higher than the 4.75% increase in subset size.
Overall this indicates that CPU usage may increase at a greater than linear scale with data size increases.

comments.author: Lily Goodyer
comments.body:
After discussions of below results with Usman and Tristan, there is an agreement that there is a requirement for increased disk size.
Final experiment:
Run 9.5% subset but with a medium-n2 with this set up (maintain 2005 partitions):

"mediumhighmem-n2": {
  "GOOGLE_HADOOP_NUM_WORKERS": "4",
  "GOOGLE_HADOOP_NUM_SECONDARY_WORKERS": "2",
  "GOOGLE_HADOOP_WORKER_MACHINE_TYPE": "n2-highmem-32",
  "GOOGLE_HADOOP_NUM_WORKER_LOCAL_SSDS": "4",
  "GOOGLE_HADOOP_MASTER_BOOT_DISK_SIZE": "4000G",
  "GOOGLE_HADOOP_WORKER_BOOT_DISK_SIZE": "8000G",
  "GOOGLE_HADOOP_AUTOSCALING_POLICY": "policy-medium",
  "E_NUM": "37",
  "E_CORES": "5",
  "E_MEMORY": "34G",
  "E_OVERHEAD": "5G"
}This adds to the medium-n2:
 "GOOGLE_HADOOP_MASTER_BOOT_DISK_SIZE": "4000G",
  "GOOGLE_HADOOP_WORKER_BOOT_DISK_SIZE": "8000G"Everything else is maintained.
Results:
consolidate_compounds time to run: 7mins, 26sec
Aggregate resource allocation:
572908705 MB seconds
 71012 vcore seconds
workers:
9 workers (4 workers, 5 secondary workers)
n2-highmem-32 : 256 GB and 32 vCores

duration in seconds: 446 seconds
Average Memory Usage = 572908705/446 = ~1284549MB (0 d.p) = 1284.549GB = ~1285GB
Average vCores Utilised = 71012/446 = ~ 159 vCores
1285GB/256 = ~5
159/32 = ~5

Conclusion/comparison:

• Node Configuration (vCPU and Memory)
medium-n2 run (with 8000GB disk per node):
9 workers total, with 256 GB memory and 32 vCores per worker (n2-highmem-32).
The workers are fewer in number and smaller in terms of CPU and memory resources.
mega-n2 run (with 500GB disk per node):
18 workers total, with 768 GB memory and 96 vCores per worker (n2-highmem-96).
These workers are more numerous and larger in terms of CPU and memory resources.

• Impact of Disk Size
Larger disks (8000 GB): Likely over-allocated in terms of storage. For most data-intensive workloads, excessive disk size doesn’t necessarily improve performance, as what matters more is disk I/O speed (how fast data can be read and written), not the storage capacity itself.
Smaller disks (500 GB): More appropriately sized, potentially reducing disk overhead and unnecessary resource allocation. Disk speed and access patterns are more crucial than disk size.

•  Number of Workers and CPU Resources
The mega-n2 run benefits significantly from having:
• More workers (18 vs. 9): This means the workload is better distributed across more nodes, leading to improved parallelism and task completion times.

• More CPU power: The mega-n2 run has 96 vCores per node (total of 18 workers), which results in a much larger computational capacity than the medium-n2 run with 32 vCores per node. This increased compute power likely accelerated the execution.


• Summary of impact:
• mega-n2 run:
Much more CPU power available across more workers, leading to faster processing of tasks, reducing the overall runtime.
The higher CPU allocation (96 vCores per worker vs. 32) reduces bottlenecks related to processing capacity.
Better parallelism with more workers and more cores means tasks can be distributed and executed simultaneously, reducing waiting times.

• Aggregate Resource Allocation
• Memory Allocation (MB seconds):
• medium-n2 run: 572,908,705 MB seconds.

• mega-n2run: 729,335,890 MB seconds.

• Although the medium-n2 run used less memory overall, the mega-n2 run used more memory per unit time, which may be indicative of faster task processing and reduced waiting times.

• vCore seconds:
• medium-n2 run: 71,012 vCore seconds.

• mega-n2 run: 90,887 vCore seconds.

• The mega-n2 run used more CPU time, but this likely corresponds to the increased number of available cores and faster completion of tasks. More cores being available means the workload was completed faster overall, even if it required more vCore seconds in aggregate.

•  Execution Time
• medum-n2 run: 7 mins 26 sec.

• mega-n2 run: 3 mins 42 sec.

• The mega-n2 run was significantly faster due to better resource allocation, with more workers, higher vCores per worker, and more efficient use of CPU and memory resources. The excessive disk space in the medium-n2 run likely introduced unnecessary overhead, whereas the more balanced configuration in the second run led to better overall performance.
Conclusion
The mega-n2 run with smaller disk space, more powerful workers (higher vCores and memory), and more workers led to better parallelization and faster processing. The larger disk space in the first run didn’t help performance but may have added some overhead. The key factors for the performance difference are the increased number of workers and their more powerful CPU and memory configurations in the second run.
HOWEVER, The failure of the previous runs when  the disk space is 100 GB suggests that while the 8000 GB disk space may have been excessive, the job likely has specific disk space requirements that exceed 100 GB.

comments.author: Lily Goodyer
comments.body:
New cluster config after discussions with Usman:
"oc-consolidate-n2": {    
"GOOGLE_HADOOP_NUM_WORKERS": "12",    "GOOGLE_HADOOP_NUM_SECONDARY_WORKERS": "10",    "GOOGLE_HADOOP_WORKER_MACHINE_TYPE": "n2-highmem-96",    "GOOGLE_HADOOP_NUM_WORKER_LOCAL_SSDS": "16",    "GOOGLE_HADOOP_MASTER_BOOT_DISK_SIZE": "250G",    "GOOGLE_HADOOP_WORKER_BOOT_DISK_SIZE": "1000G",    "GOOGLE_HADOOP_AUTOSCALING_POLICY": "policy-consolidate",    
"E_NUM": "420",   
"E_CORES": "5",    
"E_MEMORY": "33G",    
"E_OVERHEAD": "4G"  }Changes vs Mega-n2 → increased primary and secondary workers, increased worker boot disk size, and created a more flexible autoscaling policy.
Autoscaling policy:
 {    "basicAlgorithm": {      
 "cooldownPeriod": "120s",      
 "yarnConfig": {        
 "gracefulDecommissionTimeout": "0s",        
 "scaleUpFactor": 0.1      }    },    
 "id": "policy-consolidate",    
 "name": "projects/ochre-1-dead-dev-1/regions/europe-west1/autoscalingPolicies/policy-consolidate",    "secondaryWorkerConfig": {      
 "maxInstances": 25,      
 "minInstances": 10,      
 "weight": 1    },    
"workerConfig": {      
 "maxInstances": 12,      
 "minInstances": 12,      
 "weight": 1    }  }  Changes vs mega-policy → Increased scaleUpFactor to 0.1 from 0.05, and increased max instances of secondary workers to 25 (can reach 37 total workers)

Also picked up silent bug involving setting SSDs → Realised that this wasn’t actually getting set in the code, defaults were being used. Airflow cluster generator doesn’t have capacity to set SSDs. Fix has been put in by Usman to hopefully allow for future adaptation of SSDs beyond the default if required.

See this PR for all changes.

tickets.summary: Investigate OC load size increase
tickets.description:
OC full elastic indices have increased by an order of magnitude after the most recent incremental load (these now rival the size of our largest data sources like dnb and orbis).
We need to investigate the cause of this.
Start with looking at the sizes of the DocumentIndexInput files which were used to populate the full and incremental elastic indices.
We can also look at whether the delete from elastic step is operating correctly, and whether we have duplicate documents in elastic.
If the cause is not among the preceding, inspection of individual elastic documents vs their corresponding DII documents will likely be needed.
tickets.creator: Tristan Dickson
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
Causes found and documented on confluence
Establish whether we need to increase the size or number of nodes on prod elastic

tickets.summary: Setup nightly OC and OS subsets for QSB
tickets.description:
QSB () need nightly OC and OS subsets to run their downstream processes. They need DocumentIndexInput.parquet files and the corresponding elastic indicies to be populated in dev
They don’t require as many records as us, but are happy to take a sample from the DocumentIndexInput.parquet we have generated from our raw subsets going through ETL.
NB:- Not sure if we’ve decided on subset sizes and the cluster sizes required to run ETL for them, but a good rule of thumb may be that the subset size must be small enough to run each stage in less than 10 minutes on a small cluster for example. Check the subsets resulting from this are big enough for Liviu.
They would ideally like it if these compounds subsets contain the same records linked between OC and OS but have have agreed we do not need to do this straight-away.
This will involve setting up a dag for each of OC and OS which runs main of internal data feeds, nightly, to generate compounds and elastic index results from these subsets (this is to ensure QSB are testing against the latest version of data packs and QP at all times)
<data-source>_subset_raw:code location: either -
• add stage to data_download_dag.py as final stage for oc and os. Manually code stages using PE pattern.

• generate using existing etl_day.py pattern, by adding new sections to nodatagen: opencorporates_subset and opensanctions_subset. Manually synchronise these with the schedule from data download (or call trigger them from data download potentially using TriggerDagRunOperator.
stages: subset_raw
data location: prod datalake 
schedule: same schedule as data download
<data-source>_qsb_subset_dag:code location: add sections for oc and os to batch.json e.g. oc_qsb_subset_etl_full with all below stages
input source: <data-source>_raw_subset
stages:  import_raw -> ... -> extract_compounds -> load_to_elastic -> (snapshot_elastic)
data location: gs://ochre-1-dead-dev-1-data/processed/etl/iterations/nightly_subset_<yyyy-MM-dd>/opencorporates/<data_id>/…
schedule: daily

tickets.summary: ETL scripts should use the Google Cloud library
tickets.description:
This task is to refactor the ETL scripts to use the Google Cloud library for Dataproc operations instead of subprocess and gcloud commands. 
The current ETL scripts were initially designed for internal team use and rely on subprocess / gcloud for operations such as creating clusters, submitting jobs, and checking job statuses. As external teams (e.g. QSB) are now using these scripts, it is crucial to ensure they are robust, secure, and maintainable. This improvement aims to enhance functionality, adhere to best practices, and support integration with Airflow.
Objectives:
• Improve Functionality: Leveraging the Google Cloud library will provide better integration with Google Cloud Platform services and ensure the scripts are using up-to-date and efficient methods.

• Adhere to Best Practices: Using the Google Cloud library aligns with industry best practices, improving security and maintainability.

• Facilitate Airflow Integration: This refactor will enable shared functionality between the ETL scripts and our Airflow implementation, streamlining workflows and enhancing efficiency.

• Review other uses of subprocess: Replace usage of subprocess calls outside of dynamic dag where possible.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• ETL scripts use Google Cloud library
• Refactored code for cluster creation, job submission, job status checks.

• Configuration includes image version, component gateway, disk configurations, etc.

• All necessary spark configurations and properties are correctly mapped.

• Documentation:
• README is updated to reflect the new implementation.

• Testing:
• Scripts pass all tests and are deployed without issues.

• External teams can use the scripts without encountering errors related to the changes.

tickets.summary: Detailed Logging for Cluster Creation, Deletion, and Retry Processes
tickets.description:
Implement detailed logging for each step of the cluster creation, deletion, and retry processes in our Apache Airflow ETL operations. The logs should capture reasons for any errors encountered, providing comprehensive insights into the cluster lifecycle.
Currently, our logging for cluster creation tasks is minimal and does not provide sufficient information when errors occur. This makes it challenging to diagnose issues and understand the cluster's state transitions.
Requirements
• Implement logging for the initiation of cluster creation.

• Capture the state of the cluster immediately after creation.

• Implement logging for cluster deletion events.

• Capture reasons for deletion, especially when the cluster is in an ERROR state.

• Implement logging for each retry attempt in the cluster creation process.

• Capture the state of the cluster before and after each retry attempt.

• Capture detailed error messages and stack traces for any exceptions encountered during the creation, deletion, or retry processes.

• Log specific error scenarios such as InternalServerError, ResourceAvailabilityError, QuotaExceededError, NetworkError, and AuthenticationError.

• Ensure that logs are structured in a clear and consistent format.

• Include timestamps, cluster IDs, and relevant context for each log entry.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

parent.summary: Automated Incremental ETL Framework for Scheduled Live ETL refreshes

tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Detailed logs must be generated for each step of the cluster creation, deletion, and retry process.

• Logs must capture reasons for any errors encountered, including specific error messages and stack traces.

• Logs should be easily accessible through our existing logging infrastructure.

• Ensure logs are searchable and can be filtered by cluster ID, error type, and timestamps.

• Integrate the logging mechanism with the updated ETL DAG generation code and the Quantexa Airflow Helpers Module.

• Validate the logging functionality in a staging environment to ensure all required information is captured accurately.

tickets.summary: Cluster Creation Task Retry on Error State
tickets.description:
Ensure the cluster creation task can retry if it fails due to lack of resources or other errors. Implement logic to delete the cluster when it goes into an error state and retry the creation until successful.
This task focuses on enhancing our ETL operations in Apache Airflow. Currently, when a cluster enters an ERROR state, the initial cluster creation attempt fails, but it succeeds on the second retry because it finds an existing cluster with the same name. However, this process does not verify the cluster's state, leading to further issues. If the cluster is in an ERROR state, the subsequent Spark job submission to the Dataproc cluster fails, and the cluster is deleted.
To address this, we need to improve the cluster creation stage by implementing a state check after each creation attempt. If the cluster is in an ERROR state, it should be deleted, and the creation process should be retried. This loop (creation -> ERROR -> delete -> creation) should continue until a clean cluster is successfully created. The goal is to ensure that the cluster is always in a valid state before proceeding with Spark job submissions.
Common Errors Leading to ERROR State:
• Internal Server Error:
google.api_core.exceptions.InternalServerError: 500
Failed to initialize node airflow-dpr-release-10x-large-n2-dowjones-m: Component mapreduce failed to activate post-hdfs. See output in: gs://ochre-1-dead-prod-1-airflow-dpr-stg-bucket/google-cloud-dataproc-metainfo/75b3d62c-0ed5-4b38-8662-18d0fd268f62/airflow-dpr-release-10x-large-n2-dowjones-m/dataproc-post-hdfs-startup-script_output
• Resource Availability Error:
airflow.exceptions.AirflowException: 503 The zone 'projects/ochre-1-dead-prod-1/zones/europe-west1-b' does not have enough resources available to fulfill the request. '(resource type:compute)'. 14: The zone 'projects/ochre-1-dead-prod-1/zones/europe-west1-b' does not have enough resources available to fulfill the request. '(resource type:compute)'.
• Quota Exceeded Error:
google.api_core.exceptions.ResourceExhausted: 429 Quota exceeded for quota metric 'Dataproc worker CPUs' and limit 'Dataproc worker CPUs per region' in region 'europe-west1'.
• Network Error:
google.api_core.exceptions.ServiceUnavailable: 503 The network 'default' is not available. Ensure the network exists and is properly configured.
• Authentication Error:
google.api_core.exceptions.Forbidden: 403 Missing or insufficient permissions.Required code changes:
• Modify the ETL DAG code and airflow helpers to include logic for checking the state of the cluster after creation.

• Implement retry logic that deletes the cluster if it goes into an ERROR state and attempts creation again.
By implementing these changes, we will ensure that the cluster creation task in our Airflow ETL operations is more robust and can handle errors more gracefully, improving overall reliability and efficiency.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• The cluster creation task includes a retry mechanism that automatically retries if an error state is encountered.

• Flow chart of logic for all different cluster states

• The system checks the state of the cluster after each creation attempt. If the cluster is in an ERROR state, it should deleted before the next retry.

• Exponential backoff for retries
tickets.testing_required: Validate that the retry logic works as expected and handles different error scenarios.

tickets.summary: Add `qsearch` files to Data Packs [2 pts]
tickets.description:
A user would like to use search2 with data-packs
qsearch files are search field configuration files that are used by Search2 to control what fields are searchable. From Search team:  “qsearch files replace the fieldGroups  section of searchDefinitions. There is a gradle task which migrates fieldGroups into qsearch files. It tries to create Entity Search Fields wherever possible, but often won't have enough information to associate a Search Field with an Entity, so it creates Document Search Fields instead. To run it, you do this; gradlew data-source-all:generateSearchFields ."
Spoke to some teams that have used this task and are not convinced about its use and it is not fully working - could be a useful starting point but likely we will need to manually configure files. 
This ticket will involve migrating the current resolver config search configuration of each data pack to qsearch configuration. 
DEA have defined standard entity qsearch files for entity types and traversals that we should be able to use without any additional configuration and are availible via the fusion-core-search dependency. 
So we should only need to define Document search fields in Data Packs.
Links/Docs 
Link to discussion with Search team
Link to PE migration
qsearch v qmappings
qsearch defines search fields, while mappings files defines what fields are to be indexed in elastic, which is information that is needed by Search2 for queries. 
Project defines a search field that is not defined as a search field in elastic mappings → Search2 will not search this field and log an error. 
Project defines a searchable field in qmappings that is not defined in a qsearch field → Search2 will not search this field unless searched using a Free Text Search. 
Search2 docs - https://docs.quantexa.com/reference-component/2.6.5/reference/user-interface/search-2/search-introduction.html#article-main

Considerations 
Do we want to make everything searchable by default in qsearch and then recommend projects use qmappings to customise search configuration?
How do we evaluate we are not regressing and loosing any search functionality/information?
Do we contribute to the search2-dynamic.json configuration - or is this for Trials?
Do we want to try and deploy TP with Search2 data-packs as part of this ticket?

https://github.com/quantexa-internal/standard-parsers/blob/main/fusion-core-search/src/main/resources/fusion-core-config/search/parsedBusiness.qsearch
Steps
 gradlew data-source-all:generateSearchFields to migrate resolver config search fields. 
Review generated qsearch files and manually configure if required.
tickets.creator: Ciaran McFadden

• Plop-migration to add fusion-core-search dep to build.gradles

tickets.summary: Add further dataproc enhancements for improve stability and performance
tickets.description:
Recently, we have made a number of changes to our cluster properties and spark config for the upgrade to n2 machines. We may consider additional properties to further enhance our cluster configurations (Dataproc Cluster Properties):
dataproc.allow.zero.workers: Allows for cost-effective single-node clusters, ideal for lighter workloads or development environments.
dataproc.await-new-workers-service-registration Ensures that newly added nodes are fully operational before they are integrated into the cluster, enhancing stability.
dataproc.cluster.caching: Improves performance by caching frequently accessed data on the cluster, reducing read times and network traffic.
dataproc.lineage.enabled: Provides visibility into data lineage, essential for debugging and understanding the transformations within our ETL processes.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

tickets.summary: Transition from N1 to N2 Machine Types for Dataproc Clusters
tickets.description:
As part of our ongoing efforts to optimize our Big Data processing capabilities and cost-efficiency, we are planning to transition our Dataproc clusters from N1 to N2 machine types. This transition requires a careful assessment and adjustment of Dataproc and Spark settings to ensure optimal resource utilization and performance.
Background: Recent discussions and tests have indicated that the move to N2 machines could potentially reduce the size of our mega clusters by adjusting Spark memory and core settings. Preliminary suggestions include scaling down the number of nodes and utilizing preemptible instances with N2 settings to maintain or improve performance while managing costs.
Considerations:
• Cluster Size Limitation: Initially, limit cluster sizes to no more than 2000 cores, as data is partitioned to this scale. This suggests a reduction of the mega cluster size to 21 nodes from 40.

• N2 Machine Settings: Based on recommendations, use the following initial settings for N2 machines (mega):
• GOOGLE_HADOOP_NUM_WORKERS: "5"

• GOOGLE_HADOOP_NUM_SECONDARY_WORKERS: "35" (scale down if resources are not available)

• GOOGLE_HADOOP_WORKER_MACHINE_TYPE: "n2-highmem-96"

• GOOGLE_HADOOP_NUM_WORKER_LOCAL_SSDS: "16"

• Turn on --properties=dataproc:efm.spark.shuffle=primary-worker on cluster create to manage shuffle I/O efficiently.

• Resource Utilization Review: Assess the Yarn UI for both N1 and N2 machines to ensure resources are being utilized effectively with the current settings.

• Further Investigation: The unexpected behavior observed with the dataproc:efm.spark.shuffle setting necessitates further investigation to understand its impact on shuffle issues and overall cluster performance.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Conduct a detailed assessment of resource utilization on Yarn UI for N1 and N2 machine types.

• Implement the suggested settings for N2 machines and monitor performance and resource utilization.

• Investigate and resolve the encountered errors during ETL runs to ensure smooth processing.

tickets.description:
This task will involve updating the config (batch.json / ochre.conf / raw-metata.conf) to add opensanctions to the pattern and ensure ETL is working as expected, particularly on airflow. 
Before running ETL on airflow, we should ensure that we can run it from the ETL scripts in the dev environment using subsetted data, and then on airflow using the same config. We should run these tests for full mode and incremental mode; adding the stages as required.
Once this has been merged to main or another protected branch, we can promote the DAG into the prod airflow and run this on full data. The intention is to schedule the downloads and ETL to run weekly on prod.

tickets.summary: CLONE - Modify the current Airflow setup to integrate Vault secrets for airflow ETL DAGs
tickets.description:
Following the discussion with Mark, we will adop[t the approach outlined in the secure-project-pipelines, favouring it over granting direct Vault access to Airflow. Here are the steps on how it works:

• The airflow deploy pipeline sets env.AIRFLOW_ELASTIC_PASSWORD_ENCRYPTED here.

• The deploy pipeline runs a, project-defined, variable update functions here.

• Taking PE as an example, the project-defined function set the airflow password in the config file here.

• The password is in the config file here.

• These are then pushed up to the bucket here via the project defined function here.

• This config file can then be used when creating the Dataproc cluster and running the Spark job in the DAG. See here, here, and here as an example.
The previous modifications to the Airflow Vault policy have been reverted. The airflow-svc will no longer access passwords directly from Vault.
For dev, (if its not appropriate to set up a pipeline) we could create the config file with the password locally (following the same steps as above) and manually load this into the bucket, just make sure its at the correct path and in a clearly named directory so it can be cleaned up e.g. https://console.cloud.google.com/storage/browser/quantexa-ochre-1-dead-dev-1-airflow2/dag[…]-dead-dev-1&prefix=&forceOnObjectsSortingFiltering=false.
tickets.creator: Alun Meredith

tickets.summary: Add DAG-Level Properties to batch.json and update default args
tickets.description:
Update the batch.json configuration to include DAG-level properties, enhancing the management and customization of ETL processes:
• Set Schedule Interval Property for ETL DAGs: Add a schedule interval property to the ETL DAGs, optionally undefined if manual dag, allowing for flexible and clear scheduling of ETL processes within Airflow. 

• Configure UpdateMode for Incremental Datasources: Establish an explicit updateMode configuration for incremental datasources, moving away from inferring update modes based on datasource keys. This will enhance clarity and control over data processing methods.
