tickets.summary: Enhance Dataproc Efficiency: Optimize Clusters and Spark Configurations for Cost and Performance
tickets.description:
This ticket aims to thoroughly evaluate and optimize our Google Cloud Dataproc cluster configurations and management practices to enhance efficiency and cost-effectiveness. We will focus on reviewing and fine-tuning the sizes of primary and secondary worker nodes, selecting the most appropriate machine types, and incorporating Dataproc's enhanced flexibility mode and auto zone placement to ensure optimal resource utilisation. 
Additionally, this task involves refining Spark job configurations to align with the updated cluster settings, aiming for optimal performance and cost efficiency. An integral part of this optimization process also includes evaluating our current lifecycle management practices to adjust cluster sizes appropriately and minimize unnecessary cluster creation and deletion, following internal architecture (IA) recommendations.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

subtasks.summary: Add dataproc/spark guide to confluence for new changes

tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Dataproc Configuration Assessment and Optimization:
• Evaluate current cluster settings, considering the detailed configurations provided and suggestions by Greg (see   and  )

• Implement optimizations for primary and secondary worker nodes, adhering to the recommended limit of 2000-ish cores in total (cores x number of workers)

• Transition to n2 machine types across all environments, adjusting SSD configurations accordingly.

• Activate Dataproc enhanced flexibility mode and auto zone placement for better resource utilisation.

• Utilise preemptible VMs wherever feasible to reduce costs.

• Spark Configuration Optimization:
• For each cluster size (small, medium, large, mega, elastic), assess and adjust Spark settings, including executor/driver memory, memory overhead, number of cores, and the number of executor instances based on the transition from n1 to n2 machine types.

• Ensure the Spark configurations are optimised to complement the Dataproc cluster adjustments, focusing on efficiency and job performance.

• Incorporate the proof of concept provided for the transition from n1 to n2 machine types as an adjustment benchmark.

• Performance and Cost Efficiency Analysis:
• Conduct testing to monitor Dataproc job performance pre and post-optimisation.

• Analyse the cost implications of the adjustments, particularly the impact of using preemptible VMs and the updated Spark and Dataproc configurations.

• Document findings, highlighting the efficiency gains and potential cost savings achieved through optimisation.
tickets.testing_required:
• Comprehensive testing of Dataproc jobs to evaluate performance improvements and cost savings.

• Analysis and documentation of performance metrics and cost effectiveness post-implementation.

tickets.summary: Productionise Airflow ETL Implementation and Deployment
tickets.description:
This project aims to improve the implementation and deployment processes of Airflow within the Data Feeds Team. We want to make our Airflow ETL processes more efficient, reliable and maintainable. To achieve this, we will optimize batch scripts, improve CI/CD practices, manage resources effectively, and ensure comprehensive configuration management. Our goal is to create a streamlined, robust, and scalable Airflow deployment that supports our data processing needs with precision and agility.
Goals:
• Enhance ETL Batch Scripts: We will ensure that our ETL batch scripts are clear, functional, and ready for external team usage.

• Implement Notification Systems: We will set up Slack notifications to monitor Airflow ETL jobs, which will enhance our team's awareness and response capabilities.

• Optimize CI/CD Practices: We will clean up and optimize Airflow CI/CD functions to reduce redundancy and improve code maintainability.

• Dataproc Cluster Optimization: We will assess and optimize Dataproc cluster sizes and management practices to improve efficiency and reduce costs.

• Standardize Configuration Management: We will ensure consistent and effective configuration management across all datasources and tools.

• Automation and Streamlining: We will automate the derivation of environment-specific variables and other key properties to streamline operations and reduce manual overhead.

• Consolidate Configuration: We will create a unified configuration management approach that serves both Airflow and ETL scripts, enhancing consistency and ease of updates.

tickets.summary: Restructure download tasks [2 pt]
tickets.description:
We want to restructure our data download tasks, currently its set up as follows:
This means that if the download fails, the vm remains running when we set retries, which can obviously get expensive if we want to wait like a day to rerun, so we want to condense the tasks so that we can shut down/ boot up vms for each retry
The problem is that some of these stages use SSHOperators, where the task will ssh into the download vms via airflow connections
The first stage of this ticket would be to ensure that all tasks are running on bash operators. This means reliably ssh’ing into download vms.
Then condense relevant tasks into a single task.
tickets.creator: Cathal Holloway
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Restructure data downloads for each data source

• data download retries are shutting down/booting up vm’s each time

• Retries set appropriately

tickets.summary: Investigate NullPointerException in ProjectParsingUtils during Data Ingestion
tickets.description:
Caleb has reported a NullPointerException during data ingestion in version 2.5_2.0, specifically within ProjectParsingUtils.scala when mapping primaryAddress congressionalDistrict. Despite congressionalDistrict being consistently empty in the data, the exception is thrown at ProjectParsingUtils.scala:140 (see link) in the D&B data pack module, indicating a potential issue with null handling.
This issue has been reported by the team working on the client environment setup for v 2.5_2.0, but also seems to persist in the main branch (see link).
Steps to Reproduce:
• Run the D&B data pack ingestion process with version 2.5_2.0 on data where congressionalDistrict fields are empty.

• Observe the NullPointerException at ProjectParsingUtils.scala:140 (or :151 in the main branch).
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Investigate the root cause of the NullPointerException within ProjectParsingUtils.

• Assess the bug’s impact on data ingestion and the scale of affected data.

• Propose a plan to fix the bug, considering:
• Immediate hotfix within the current ticket if the fix is straightforward and low risk.

• Creation of a new ticket for a more comprehensive solution if the issue is complex or requires significant changes.
Ensure that the solution includes handling empty fields in a way that prevents such exceptions.

tickets.summary: Implement Incremental Mode for OpenCorporates in Data Packs
tickets.description:
We need to enhance the OpenCorporates data source within our Data Packs product by adding an incremental mode to improve data processing efficiency. This involves configuring and executing various ETL stages to support incremental updates.
Key tasks include: (as mentioned in the docs)
• Configuring etl.conf for incremental mode, ensuring updateMode is enabled for relevant document types.

• Implementing CreateCaseClassDelta to generate deltas for incremental updates.

• Adjusting cleansing, and loading configurations to support incremental processing.

• Implementing reprocessing and deletes script as per the incremental mode requirements.This effort aims to reduce processing time and resource consumption by updating only changed data, rather than re-processing the entire dataset.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Incremental Update Capability: The system must support incremental updates, processing only the data that has changed since the last update cycle.

• Data Accuracy: Incrementally updated data packs must maintain high data integrity and accuracy, with all changes correctly reflected.

• Efficiency Improvements: Demonstrable reduction in processing time and resource usage for incremental updates compared to full data re-processing.

• Error Handling: Robust error handling and logging for the incremental update process, with clear reporting of issues encountered during data processing.

• Documentation: Comprehensive documentation of the incremental mode configuration, execution steps, and troubleshooting guide for future reference.
tickets.testing_required:
• Test data cleansing and aggregation stages for handling incremental updates, including scenario testing for data updates, additions, and deletions.

• Ensure that the system can handle reprocessing and deletes accurately, with proper updates to aggregated records and deletion of obsolete records.

• Performance testing to compare processing times and resource usage between full and incremental updates.

This will involve the implementation of a standardized method for both updating the raw data locations in the Git configuration and fetching these locations by using the runId as a key during the ETL stages.

tickets.summary: Modify the current Airflow setup to integrate Vault secrets for airflow ETL DAGs
tickets.description:
Following the discussion with Mark, we will adopt the approach outlined in the secure-project-pipelines, favouring it over granting direct Vault access to Airflow. Here are the steps on how it works:

• The airflow deploy pipeline sets env.AIRFLOW_ELASTIC_PASSWORD_ENCRYPTED here.

• The deploy pipeline runs a, project-defined, variable update functions here.

• Taking PE as an example, the project-defined function set the airflow password in the config file here.

• The password is in the config file here.

• These are then pushed up to the bucket here via the project defined function here.

• This config file can then be used when creating the Dataproc cluster and running the Spark job in the DAG. See here, here, and here as an example.
The previous modifications to the Airflow Vault policy have been reverted. The airflow-svc will no longer access passwords directly from Vault.
For dev, (if its not appropriate to set up a pipeline) we could create the config file with the password locally (following the same steps as above) and manually load this into the bucket, just make sure its at the correct path and in a clearly named directory so it can be cleaned up e.g. https://console.cloud.google.com/storage/browser/quantexa-ochre-1-dead-dev-1-airflow2/dag[…]-dead-dev-1&prefix=&forceOnObjectsSortingFiltering=false.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

tickets.summary: Unresolved Dependencies Despite Correct Nexus Repository Configuration
tickets.description:
Encountering unresolved dependency issues in a Scala project. Despite updating ivysettings.xml with the correct Nexus repository (quantexa-saas-snapshots), the build process is failing to find dependencies com.quantexa.trials.platform#data-source-all-shadow-dependency_2.12;AC-5928-SNAPSHOT and com.quantexa.trials.platform#data-source-all-shadow-projects_2.12;AC-5928-SNAPSHOT. The issue persists despite verifying and updating ivysettings.xml with correct repository settings, ensuring the Nexus repository contains required artifacts, and restarting the cluster. 
This was discussed on the following qcp-support thread.
• There is a possibility of permission issues or incorrect handling of snapshot repositories in the ivysettings.xml.

• Suggestions include modifying the resolver in ivysettings.xml to better handle snapshots, possibly using checkmodified and changingPattern properties.

tickets.summary: Standardise Data Source Naming in Ochre Buckets
tickets.description:
We've observed inconsistencies in the naming patterns of data sources in the Ochre buckets, leading to challenges in generalising code for ETL processes. This ticket aims to standardise these patterns to align with our established conventions.
Current Pattern:
• For some data sources like Orbis: gs://quantexa-ochre-data-lake-${datasource}/raw/rolling/airflow-downloads/xxx

• For others, an extra ${datasource} folder is added: gs://quantexa-ochre-data-lake-${datasource}/raw/rolling/${datasource}/airflow-downloads/xxx
Examples:
• ICIJ: gs://quantexa-ochre-data-lake-icij/raw/static/20220503/

• D&B: gs://quantexa-ochre-data-lake-dnb/raw/static/dnb/airflow-downloads/cmpbol/increment/

• Orbis: gs://quantexa-ochre-data-lake-orbis/raw/rolling/airflow-downloads/2023-10-10_00-04-11-435355/

• OpenCorporates: gs://quantexa-ochre-data-lake-opencorporates/raw/rolling/opencorporates/airflow-downloads/full/2023-12-27-16-31/

• GRID: gs://quantexa-ochre-data-lake-grid/raw/rolling/grid/airflow-downloads/full/2023-12-01/
Note: Bucket does not exist for Dow Jones
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Review the current naming conventions across all data sources.

• Propose a standardised pattern, eliminating unnecessary folder levels.

• Plan and execute the restructuring of the data directories to conform to the new standard.

• Update any ETL scripts to reflect these changes.

• Verify consistency across all data sources and ensure smooth operation of the ETL processes.

tickets.summary: MVP Implementation of Airflow v2 DAG generation
tickets.description:
As a user, I want to input a batch.json file and have the system dynamically generate a functional ETL DAG for the ICIJ data source, aligning with PE standards and serving as a foundational model for future data packs.
Develop a MVP for processing the ICIJ data source using Apache Airflow v2, focusing initially on creating a static DAG for some stages of the ICIJ ETL. This static DAG will adhere to current practices in PE and provide a clear understanding of how to structure the JSON schema for dynamic DAG generation. 
It will also serve as a baseline for unit testing the JSON-to-DAG conversion logic, ensuring that the dynamically generated DAG for the ICIJ batch.json mirrors the functionality and structure of the static DAG. The MVP aims to establish an efficient workflow, test the integration of Quantexa Airflow v2 operators, and set the stage for scalable expansion to more complex data packs.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

tickets.summary: Implement Patched Gradle Version in CI/CD Pipeline for Enhanced Stability and Compliance
tickets.description:
We've identified a recurring issue with our Gradle builds in the CI/CD pipeline, specifically on Jenkins. The builds are failing due to the Gradle build daemon disappearing unexpectedly. After consulting with QCP Support (thread), it's been suggested that we switch to a patched version of Gradle available in the QCP Nexus repository. This patched version addresses several known bugs and is in line with QCP best practices.
To implement this, we need to update our Jenkinsfile (including this function and this stage) to customise the Gradle distribution URL in the gradle-wrapper.properties files across various project directories. This change ensures that our builds use the patched Gradle version, potentially resolving current build issues and improving overall build stability.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• The Jenkinsfile is updated with the script provided by QCP Support for customizing the Gradle distribution.

• All CI/CD pipelines successfully use the patched Gradle version from the QCP Nexus repository.

• No instances of the Gradle build daemon disappearing unexpectedly in any of the builds post-implementation.

• Confirmation that the change adheres to QCP best practices and does not introduce any new issues.
tickets.testing_required:
• Validate that the updated Jenkinsfile correctly modifies the gradle-wrapper.properties in all specified directories.

• Ensure that builds using the patched Gradle version complete successfully without the daemon disappearing.

• Test builds on different branches, including the intelligent subsetting branch, to confirm consistent behavior across the board.

tickets.summary: Incremental Mode ETL Processes (OC)
tickets.description:
This task is focused on preparing and planning for the future implementation of ETL processes in incremental mode, taking into account the newly developed dynamic DAG generation feature. The goal is to adapt the ETL system to efficiently process and update data incrementally, reducing resource usage and processing time compared to full mode processing. 
This involves identifying suitable data sources for incremental processing, determining the appropriate logic for data extraction and loading, and integrating these processes with the dynamic DAG generation capabilities of Airflow v2. A key aspect of this task will be to establish criteria for determining when to use incremental mode versus full mode processing.
Full mode will still be run in the first instance (as the base increment) or under other circumstances (such as new versions of DP) where we may want to trigger this manually.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• A comprehensive plan for incremental mode ETL, including data source identification, processing logic, and integration with dynamic DAGs.

• A basic design for a prototype to test incremental processing, to be developed as part of future work in this area.

• The initial focus should be running this for OC, but the design should be compatible with other data sources.
tickets.testing_required:
• Design and Strategy Review: Evaluate the proposed design and strategies for implementing incremental mode to ensure they align with overall data processing goals and system capabilities.

• Prototype Testing (To be determined): Once a prototype is developed, conduct tests to assess the efficiency, accuracy, and reliability of the incremental mode processing.

tickets.summary: Dynamic DAG Generation from JSON for ICIJ
tickets.description:
This task focuses on developing a system to dynamically generate Apache Airflow DAGs from JSON configurations, integrating with existing Airflow v2 operators. The goal is to create a flexible and scalable ETL process where DAG structures can be defined in a JSON format, allowing for rapid adjustments and deployment without extensive manual coding. 
This system will utilise the enhanced capabilities of Airflow v2, including the use of Quantexa Dataproc Operators, to streamline ETL processes. Key considerations include ensuring compatibility with various data sources, maintaining a high level of security in data processing, and integrating this functionality seamlessly with the existing Airflow ecosystem.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

subtasks.summary: JSON Configuration Design and Implementation
Development of JSON-to-DAG Conversion Logic
Integration Testing with Airflow v2 Operators
Performance and Security Validation
Alignment with PE and collaboration with ext teams

tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Successful generation of functional DAGs from JSON configurations, with accurate reflection of all specified parameters.

• Seamless integration of these DAGs with existing Airflow v2 operators, particularly Quantexa Dataproc Operators, without any loss of functionality or performance issues.

• System stability and performance consistency, even under high load scenarios with multiple dynamic DAGs.

• Adherence to all specified security standards and protocols in the DAG generation and execution process.
tickets.testing_required:
• Functional Testing: Validate the correct conversion of JSON configurations into executable DAGs, ensuring that all specified parameters and dependencies are accurately reflected.

• Integration Testing: Test the integration of dynamically generated DAGs with existing Airflow v2 operators, ensuring seamless functionality across various ETL tasks.

• Load Testing: Assess the system’s performance under various loads, particularly focusing on scenarios with a large number of dynamically generated DAGs.

• Security Testing: Ensure that the DAG generation process adheres to security protocols, particularly in handling sensitive data and configuration information.

tickets.summary: Transitioning ETL processes to Quantexa Airflow v2 Operators for ICIJ
tickets.description:
This task involves transitioning ETL processes, previously redesigned Python scripts, and the current Airflow v1 implementation in on-demand environments, to leverage standard Airflow v2 operators. The focus is on the Quantexa Dataproc Operators, which extend Airflow's capabilities for creating and managing Dataproc clusters and submitting Spark jobs. This shift aligns with the PE library standards and emphasise avoiding custom setups in Data Feeds, as per organisational directives. The process includes:
• Adapting script logic to leverage Quantexa-specific settings, such as encrypt_key for configuration decoding and handling project-specific JAR file paths.

• Ensuring configuration structures mirror examples like "green-1-accelerators-3.conf" to utilise shared library operators effectively.

• Incorporating Quantexa Dataproc Submit Job Operator and Local Submit Spark Operator for localised and cloud-based ETL processes.

• Aligning with best practices around configuration and deployment, including using prod/dev namespaces and integrating with the generic AF CI pipeline.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

subtasks.summary: Integration and Refactoring for Airflow v2 Compatibility
Configuration Standardisation and Testing in Multiple Environments
Performance Benchmarking and Compliance Review
Documentation, Knowledge Transfer, and Feedback Mechanism

tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Successful integration and operation with the ICIJ data source, ensuring the ETL process runs efficiently in full mode using Quantexa Dataproc Operators.

• Compliance with organisational best practices and standards, including the avoidance of custom setups unique to Data Feeds.

• Clear documentation and evidence of successful test cases in both Ochre and Solar environments.

• Feedback and sign-off from relevant internal teams, confirming the alignment with the PE library standards and organisational objectives.
tickets.testing_required:
• Ochre and Solar Testing: Ensure that the redesigned ETL processes work seamlessly with both Ochre and Solar. This includes verifying the successful execution of DAGs, proper resource allocation in Dataproc clusters, and accurate data processing.

• Compatibility Testing with Existing Data Sources: Validate the integration with the ICIJ data source in full mode, ensuring that the new setup correctly processes the data without loss or corruption.

• Performance Metrics Evaluation: Monitor and record performance metrics such as processing time, resource usage, and error rates to compare against the baseline established with previous Airflow v1 setups.

Generate a functional dynamic DAG that works for ICIJ

tickets.summary: Integrating IS into restructured codebase (main)
tickets.description:
To integrate the current scripts into the restructured codebase in the main branch, we need to complete the following steps within a new branch off main called AC-5714-integrating-is-into-current-restructured-codebase:
• Rename all occurrences of BvD to Orbis in the code or config.

• Decide on the location for Intelligent Subsetting within data-source-orbis in the repo
• Copy across the current code into etl-xxx and orbis-xxx; the latter having the former as a dependency.

• Modify or create new modules or packages as needed.

• Expand the current ETL config to allow the subsetting to be set, similar to the CreateSubset feature or other config options such as partitionByFields.
The choice of the name xxx is arbitrary (e.g. orbis-subsetting) since these modules will likely be absorbed into the current ones in the future. Whatever you pick should be the same for the data-source-orbis and etl-libraries sub-modules. I will use subsetting in the name when referencing the sub-modules below, but you may pick anything appropriate.
Some of the code will be generic enough to be added to the etl-subsetting to share it across multiple datapacks. 
Otherwise, we want to keep all the scripts in the same folder in contrast with the current scripts e.g with IntelligentSubsettingGraphScriptEng.scala located in a different folder to the others with the group com.quantexa.subsetting.graph.scripting.eng. All the scripts should have the group com.quantexa.etl.libraries.subsetting or com.quantexa.orbis.subsetting.
After adding these changes, the folder structure of the data packs codepack should look like:
• Current structure:
├── data-source-common
│   ├── common-etl
│   ├── common-extractors
│   ├── common-model
│   ├── config
├── data-source-orbis
│   ├── config
│   ├── orbis-etl
│   ├── orbis-extractors
│   ├── orbis-generator
│   ├── orbis-migration
│   ├── orbis-model
│   ├── orbis-subsetting
... (other data packs)
├── etl-libraries
│   ├── etl-config
│   ├── etl-stages
│   ├── etl-test
│   ├── etl-utils
│   ├── etl-subsetting
...
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

parent.summary: Integration of Intelligent Subsetting into Data Packs Codebase [1 pts]

tickets.acceptance_criteria: The code should be integrated into the appropriate modules following the structure above. The user should be able to configure the intelligent sub-setting options from the ETL config (i.e the xxx.conf files).

In previous versions before 2.5_2.0, the version field existed only in BvD (Orbis) and Dnb models, which would be populated from modelVersion option in the ETL config (with a default value of “1”),

In the latest version, this option was added to all the datapacks to allow the migration code to function. We will need to backport this to previous versions of datapacks in a similar manner to BvD and Dnb.

tickets.summary: Introduce feature to generators for entity resolution across data sources
tickets.description:
David Vella Zarb from the data engineering team has created a PR to add the capability of passing in entities to the Orbis and ICIJ generators. The intent is to ensure that documents generated from these sources can resolve to the same entities. This would help in situations where we are working with data from different sources but want them to link to the same entities.
David has asked our team to review this.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• The introduced methods should allow users to pass in a set of predefined entities.

• These changes should be non-breaking, ensuring existing functionality is not impacted.

• Documentation should be updated to reflect the new feature.

• Existing unit tests should pass, and new tests should be added to cover this feature.

Despite proper implementation, the Create Case Class step doesn't appear to pick up the subset files, only the full files. Users have not been able to find any reference to subset in the CreateCaseClass source code. The issue also affects the parquetRoot parameter, which should point to the directory containing the subset parquet files.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria: The Create Case Class step should read from the subset files when subsetMode is configured properly and a valid 3-letter ISO Alpha-3 country code is provided in countriesToSubset.

tickets.summary: Revert orbisIdNumber Field to bvdIdNumber in Document Data Model
tickets.description:
During recent updates, we changed a field name from bvdIdNumber to orbisIdNumber. However, it has been brought to our attention that orbisId and bvdId are two distinct entities. Despite the rebranding from bvd to orbis, they retained the bvdIdNumber and introduced orbisId as a separate entity. Therefore, to maintain the integrity and correctness of our data model, we have decided to revert the field name back to bvdIdNumber.
In addition to the root level changes, we should also consider renaming fields that are part of nested structures to maintain consistency across the data model. These fields include but are not limited to branches.branchOrbisIdNumber, subsidiaries.subsidiaryOrbisIdNumber, and shareholders.shareholderOrbisIdNumber. The consensus is to rename these fields as well, aligning them with the corrected bvdIdNumber designation to ensure uniformity and avoid confusion.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

subtasks.summary: Revert orbisIdNumber  -> bvdIdNumber for data-packs 2.5_2.0 release
Revert orbisIdNumber  -> bvdIdNumber for quantexa-documentation

tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Identify all instances of the orbisIdNumber field in the document data model, including nested structures such as legalInfo.orbisIdNumber, branches.branchOrbisIdNumber, subsidiaries.subsidiaryOrbisIdNumber, and shareholders.shareholderOrbisIdNumber.

• Update all instances of orbisIdNumber to bvdIdNumber ensuring consistency across the data model.

• Create a new PR targeting the upcoming release branch with the necessary changes.

• Update the documentation to reflect this change in the data model, and create a new PR for the documentation update as well.

• Ensure that no functionalities are broken due to this change; all related functionalities should work as expected with the bvdIdNumber field.

tickets.summary: Consolidate Green and Solar Jenkinsfile Pipelines
tickets.description:
Based on the recent discussions with QCP support, we want to merge the Jenkinsfile pipelines in the Green and Solar environments. The aim is to streamline the CI/CD processes, making it easier to test changes without the need to raise support for separate testing in the Solar environment. 
For this work, we need clarify the existing differences between the Green and Solar implementations, and to identify the potential challenges and benefits of merging the pipelines.
The result will be a single Jenkinsfile in the secure-project-pipelines and the current one in data-packs i.e incicd/integration-data-pack-module/Jenkinsfile (used for the green environment) will be deleted. 
• Should module calls be series or parallel?

• Should it be a multi-branch pipeline (like green)?
Dependency on cloud for testing secure pipelines.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• The green pipeline should be switched over to use the Jenkinsfile in secure-project-pipelines

• Both the green and solar pipelines should have similar “Build with Parameters” options, although there may be differences on Solar due to the nature of the pipeline there.
tickets.testing_required: Testing on green and solar Jenkins, ensuring all the stages of the pipeline are still functioning as expected.

tickets.summary: Flatten Data Packs multi-project
tickets.description:
At the moment, each data pack directory is a multi-project in its own right. We use composite builds to compile the etl-libraries into each multi-project.
We should convert data-packs to a single multiproject. Each data pack should still publish the same artifacts and have dependency on etl-libraries.
Structure should go from
├── etl-libraries
│   ├── etl-config
│   │   └── src
│   ├── etl-stages

│   └── settings.gradle

│   │   │   └── src
│   │   ├── orbis-extractors
│   │   │   └── src
│   │   ├── orbis-model
│   │   │   └── src
│   │   └── build.gradle
│   ├── build.gradle
│   └── settings.gradle
├── build.gradle
└── settings.gradleto
├── etl-libraries
│   ├── etl-config
│   │   └── src
│   ├── etl-stages
│   │   └── src
│   ├── etl-test
│   │   └── src
│   ├── etl-utils
│   │   └── src
│   ├── build.gradle
│   └── settings.gradle
├── data-source-orbis
│   ├── orbis-etl
│   │   └── src
│   ├── orbis-extractors

│   ├── orbis-generator

│   ├── orbis-migration

├── data-source-dnb
│   ├── dnb-etl
│   │   └── src
│   ├── dnb-extractors
│   │   └── src
│   ├── dnb-model

└── settings.gradleNote that this will require moving the etl-orbis-etl-common, data-generator, etl-orbis-models-common and data-fusion-migration modules into submodules within data-source-<datapack>.
• “Data-packs” repo becomes a single multiproject with top-level data-source-x modules in above format. Similar to trials-platform.

• Single data-source-common for all data-packs, like trials-platform

• etl-orbis-etl-common classes can be merged into orbis-etl

• etl-orbis-models-common classes can be merged into orbis-model

• etl-orbis-scripts, etc can be removed

• data-pack-x/cicd can be removed as these are unused and replaced with the top-level cicd

• data-pack-x/config can be removed as these are unused

• whitesource - investigate whether we need it

Top-level gradle files:
• Important gradle files within each data-pack are gradle/libraries.gradle, gradle/release.gradle, gradle.properties - these will need "squashing"
• Make a note of design decisions, e.g “group” name in gradle.properties

• build.gradle , settings.gradle can take any data pack's (e.g data-pack-orbis/build.gradle) and use as the top-level one

Data-source gradle files:
• ETL libraries will no longer be an external dependency as they’re in the same multi-project
• api("com.quantexa.etl-libraries:etl-config_$scalaVersion:$version") → api project(":etl-libraries:etl-stages")

• Can remove all the composite stuff for etl libraries

Jenkinsfiles:
• Won’t need to move

comments.author: Usman Kayani
comments.body:
You can use this to extract any output document data model changes and output to a file:

import findspark
findspark.init()

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
import os
import sys
import shutil

DATA_PATH = 'C:/trials-platform/trials-platform-scripts/output/data'

def data_source_path(data_source):
    return f"{DATA_PATH}/{data_source}"

def get_schema(
        data_source: str,
        src_path: str = f"DocumentDataModel/CleansedDocumentDataModel.parquet",
        path: str = None
) -> None:
    file_path = f"{data_source_path(data_source)}/{src_path}"
    
    df = spark.read.parquet(file_path)

    orig_stdout = sys.stdout
    if 'DocumentDataModel' in src_path:
        file_name = f"{data_source}-output-model-format.txt"

    if path:
        os.makedirs(path, exist_ok=True)
    file_name = f'{path}/{file_name}' if path else file_name
    sys.stdout = open(file_name, 'w')

    df.printSchema()

    sys.stdout.close()
    sys.stdout = orig_stdout

data_source = 'icij'
spark = SparkSession.builder.appName('Parquet Reader').getOrCreate()
get_schema(data_source, path=f'print_schema/{data_source}')

comments.author: Usman Kayani
comments.body:
I reached out to  for expert guidance on several key aspects, including integrating QPython for its rich environment, and understanding Quantexa's approach to dependency management using Poetry. I also sought advice on implementing unit testing to maintain high code quality.
Key Points from Tejan's Response:• Python Best Practices: Tejan advises starting with Quantexa's Python docs and internal Confluence documentation to acquaint ourselves with best practices.

• Tooling: Standard industry tools like Pytest for testing, Black for code formatting, and Mypy for type checking are in use.

• Version Control and Dependency Management: Tejan clarified that Git is the go-to for version control. Poetry is favored as a dependency manager and build tool, offering superior resolution and configuration over standard pip.
Next Steps:• Consult the provided documentation for Python best practices (link) and internal confluence docs with the recommended tooling for developing high quality python code (link).

• Incorporate Pytest, Black, and Mypy into our development workflow.

• Utilize Git for version control and Poetry for managing dependencies.

• For further assistance beyond the documentation, reach out to Tejan, especially for setting up the project with Poetry and other recommended tooling.

tickets.summary: Fix Data Generator and Functional Test Issues for OpenCorporates
tickets.description:
There are observed inconsistencies with the data generator's output for the name and uid fields, resulting in values like org.scalacheck.Gen$$anon$5@75581e0. Additionally, there seem to be concerns with the functionality of the OC (OpenCorporates) functional test that require investigation and potential fixes.
Details:
• The name and uid fields in the data generator are producing inappropriate values, possibly due to improper sampling from the generator.

• The OC functional test appears to have discrepancies, leading to concerns about its reliability and accuracy.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

comments.author: Usman Kayani
comments.body:
Changes:
• Rectified the data generator's issue related to the name and uid fields, which previously manifested as values such as "org.scalacheck.Gen$$anon$5@75581e0".

• Updated DocumentDataInput.json and ensured its consistency across multiple test runs using a personalized script.
Observations:
• Detected potential complications with the DiffDocumentIndexInputsScript utilized within the functional test. This script is designed to contrast the current DocumentDataInput with the version maintained under version control. It is supposed to generate a DocumentIndexInputComparison.parquet file when discrepancies are observed, and the test is set up to check for this file's presence.
However, despite deliberately introducing inconsistencies to the DocumentDataInput.json, the test consistently showed a pass. Directly comparing the two parquet files indicated evident differences, leading to the suspicion that the DiffDocumentIndexInputsScript might not be producing the anticipated output file.
Future Prospects:
The current version of DocumentDataInput.json has been validated against numerous test runs and has consistently exhibited the expected output. A more comprehensive probe into the wider issues associated with functional tests has been slated under ticket AC-5501.

tickets.summary: Investigate Unreliable Functional Tests and Potential Improvements
tickets.description:
There have been concerns raised about the reliability of the functional tests, especially when working with DocumentIndexInput.json. The tests appear to be inconsistent, which could be slowing down the development process. Suggestions have been made regarding potential improvements and changes to the testing process to make it more efficient and reliable.
Details:
• The slowTests have been reported as very flaky and inconsistent, making them potentially unreliable. With numerous schema changes being introduced, there are concerns that these tests might hinder the development process. It was also highlighted that similar tests were removed for ICIJ in a recent release due to related issues.

• A proposal has been made to possibly relax the slowTest requirement, turning it into a separate script instead of a core part of the development process. The ICIJ functional test, the first of its kind, had undergone numerous changes since its creation, which might be a source of its current unreliability.

• In the future, we might specify checking against the version-controlled file as an acceptance criterion for relevant tickets instead of having it in the slowTest.

• The reliability of these tests should be a priority. We need to ensure that our development process isn't hampered by unreliable tests.
Tasks:
• Investigate why the slowTests are currently unreliable.

• Examine the potential use of a script for generating and comparing JSON data.

• Consider the possibility of transforming the slowTest requirement into a separate script.

• Review the changes made to the ICIJ functional test and other datapack functional tests to identify potential sources of unreliability.

Hi Usman, 
You have requested and now been allocated a Docker Desktop licence. Please follow the below instructions;

 net localgroup docker-users "azuread\username" /add
Please follow these steps to ensure you are using Docker Desktop in a legally licensed manner;
 
• Install/re-install/check Docker's installed (it might have been uninstalled recently by Heimdal)

• You should have received an email entitled "[EXTERNAL] [Docker] You've been invited to join quantexa", from no-reply@notify.docker.com, with either mine, Vasco's, Kam's, Egan's or Charles’s username in it (qdaveburt, vascocoelhouk, qkammiah, qeganloughran), follow the link in that email. (NOTE: If the email does not match this description, and does not list one of these usernames it might be phishing – be careful and seek advice from Infosec if unsure)

• If not, please create a new docker account and associate it to your quantexa email, we suggest a username format of q<username> e.g. "qdaveburt".

• You'll receive another email from Docker, verify your email address. (You may need to click through and re-enter your password a couple of times).

Please note that we are using Docker for licensing Docker Desktop on our Quantexa laptops only, we are not using Docker Hub to store Docker images. These will continued to be stored in our GCP based Docker registries. I.e. you are not permitted to upload Docker images to Docker hub.

tickets.summary: [TIMEBOX] Review of null handling in Data Packs codebase
tickets.description:
As part of our ongoing efforts to improve code quality and reduce runtime errors, we've identified a need for a comprehensive review of our handling of null values within the Data Packs codebase.
This need was highlighted by recent NullPointerExceptions that occurred due to the removal of the 'quantAs' function. Although we've addressed the specific instances that caused these exceptions, this may be indicative of a larger issue within our codebase.

The goal of this task is to:
• Perform an audit of our current codebase to identify areas where we may not be adequately handling null values.

• Develop a consistent strategy for handling null values across our codebase. This strategy should prevent NullPointerExceptions and ensure that our code is as robust and reliable as possible.
It's important that we tackle this task in a systematic way, focusing not only on eliminating existing problems but also on preventing future ones.
We should force null handling at compile time, so that NullPointerExceptions are prohibited at the type level.
Research if there’s best practice in PE/Fusion/ER Accelerate that we can use.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Codebase Audit: Completion of a comprehensive audit on null handling in the current Data Packs codebase. This will involve reviewing all segments of the code where null values can potentially be a problem, and creating a report detailing all areas where null handling may be inadequate or missing.

• Strategy Development: Formulation of a robust and consistent strategy for identifying problematic areas and handling nulls. This strategy should be documented, shared, and reviewed by the team for feedback and approval. It will serve as the guiding document for handling null values throughout the codebase.

• Refactoring: Upon approval of the strategy, refactoring of identified problematic areas should be carried out according to the newly developed strategy. This includes unit tests, regression tests and edge case tests to ensure that the refactoring has been successful, and has not introduced new issues or adversely affected the system's performance.
tickets.testing_required:
• Unit Testing: Test the updated components individually to ensure they handle null values correctly. Each method that has been changed should have corresponding unit tests demonstrating the correct handling of null values.

• Integration Testing: After the unit tests pass, integration tests should be performed to verify that the updated components interact correctly with other parts of the system. This can help identify any unforeseen issues arising from the changes.

• Regression Testing: Perform full regression testing to ensure existing functionalities have not been broken. This should include running all previous tests that didn't involve null inputs to ensure they still pass.

• Edge Case Testing: Test edge cases where null values might be deeply nested or in other non-obvious places. This can ensure that even hidden or unexpected null values are handled correctly.

tickets.summary: Update Jenkinsfile on Solar to Specify Data Packs for Build
tickets.description:
We need to update the Jenkinsfile on Solar to allow us to specify the data packs that we want to build. This will provide us with more control and flexibility over our deployment process, especially considering the structure of our data packs mono-repository.
Current Jenkinsfile:
Our current Jenkinsfile has the following properties and pipeline stages:
properties([
  parameters([
    string(name: 'BRANCH', description: 'Which branch to deploy?'),
    string(name: 'VERSION', description: 'What version tag to deploy with e.g. 1.4.0-SNAPSHOT? Must end with -SNAPSHOT. Leave blank to use version from settings.gradle in datapack repository'),
    choice(name: 'gradleVersion', choices: ['gradle-6.6', 'gradle-7.4'],  description: 'Gradle executable to use')
  ])
])

// Pipeline stages...
Data Packs Mono-Repository Structure:
Our data packs mono-repository has a specific structure, with etl-libraries as a gradle multiproject and each data pack (like data-pack-bvd) also being a gradle multiproject. The parent directory is a gradle composite project with includeBuild for the above modules. This allows us to compose the data packs with the shared "etl-libraries" during development while keeping it as an external dependency in the published artifacts.
Proposed Changes:
We propose to add a new parameter that allows us to specify the data packs that we want to build. This could be a string parameter where we can list the names of the data packs, or a choice parameter if there are only a few options. The new parameter should be used in the appropriate pipeline stages to control which data packs are built.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• The Jenkinsfile on Solar has been updated to include a new parameter for specifying the data packs to build.

• The new parameter is used in the appropriate pipeline stages to control which data packs are built.
tickets.testing_required: Test the new Jenkinsfile script by specifying particular data pack(s) to build. Verify that only the specified data pack(s) are built and that the build process completes successfully.

comments.author: Usman Kayani
comments.body:
Differences between repartition and partitionBy in Spark, and why using both shouldn't throw an invalid config error:
• Purpose:
• repartition: The repartition method is used to reshuffle the data in a DataFrame across a specified number of partitions. This is typically done to optimize the parallelism of subsequent operations on the DataFrame. For example, if you know you're about to perform a wide transformation (e.g., a join), you might repartition your data first to avoid shuffling during the join.

• partitionBy: The partitionBy method is used during DataFrame writes to specify the columns by which the output data should be partitioned when it's saved to disk. For example, when saving data in Parquet format, you might partition by date so that each date's data is saved in a separate directory.

• Operational Stage:
• repartition: This is an action that takes place in-memory while your Spark job is running. It affects the distribution of data across partitions in the Spark cluster.

• partitionBy: This affects how data is laid out on disk. It doesn't change the in-memory representation of the DataFrame.

• Use Case:
• repartition: You would use repartition to optimize the performance of Spark operations.

• partitionBy: You would use partitionBy to optimize the layout of data on disk, which can be useful for downstream applications or for optimizing read performance.
Given these differences:
• Having both repartition and partitionBy in a Spark job is not contradictory. One optimizes the in-memory layout for Spark operations (repartition), and the other optimizes the on-disk layout for storage and read operations (partitionBy).

• Using both in tandem can be beneficial. For instance, you might repartition before a join to ensure data is co-located appropriately, and then when you save the result to disk, you might partitionBy a particular column to ensure that downstream reads are efficient.
In our provided implementation:
• The repartition step optimizes the in-memory layout based on the configuration.

• The partitionByField function (from the previous discussions) partitions data when saving it to disk based on certain fields.
Given this, there's no inherent conflict between the two, and having both in the configuration shouldn't be considered invalid. It's all about ensuring that the Spark job is efficient both during computation and when saving/loading data.

comments.author: Usman Kayani
comments.body:
Key Questions
• Return Value:
• NullifyColumn: This function simply manipulates the DataFrame in-memory by setting specific columns to null and returns the transformed DataFrame. It doesn't interact with the file system or require any external paths.

• PartitionByField: On the other hand, to achieve the partitioning based on field values, we are using the DataFrameWriter.partitionBy method. This method doesn't just transform the DataFrame in-memory; it writes out the partitioned data to the file system, typically in a Parquet format. As such, it requires an output path.

• Output Path Requirement: This brings us to a critical question: How should we handle the output path for PartitionByField? Do we want to:
• Hard-code a default path?

• Make it a mandatory parameter for the user to provide?

• Or perhaps pull it from a configuration file?

• Data Types:
• Unlike NullifyColumn, PartitionByField has restrictions on the data types of columns we can partition by. Specifically, array types and possibly numeric types might be invalid. How strict do we want to be with these data type restrictions?

• Impact on Subsequent ETL Steps:
• Given that PartitionByField writes data out and doesn't just return a transformed DataFrame, this might have implications for subsequent ETL steps. Should we read the partitioned data back into a DataFrame for further processing? Or will this be the final step in our ETL for datasets that use this transformation?

• Performance Implications:
• Writing partitioned data to the file system can be IO-intensive and might impact the performance of our ETL, especially for large datasets. Do we have any performance benchmarks or tests to ensure this doesn't adversely affect our processing times?

tickets.summary: Correcting ID Generation and Associate Handling Data Generator
tickets.description:
The DowJones data generator is designed to generate random IDs for 'personEntityId' and 'relatedPersonEntityID'. This random generation, though, is resulting in an issue where 'associateType' is null in the data derived from these randomly generated IDs, whereas 'associateType' is correctly populated when using the real data. This discrepancy appears to be a result of mismatches in ID generation during join and groupBy operations performed in the CreateCaseClass.scala script.
In the real data, the 'personEntityId' and 'relatedPersonEntityID' likely follow a specific structure or pattern that aligns with the rest of the data. The alignment of these IDs is critical during the join operation, which relies on these IDs to combine the 'rawDowJonesParquet' and 'dowJonesEntityTypes' data sources. Properly aligned IDs ensure correct population of the 'associateType' field.
However, in the case of randomly generated data, the structural coherence of these IDs is lost. This causes mismatches during join and groupBy operations, as the operations aren't able to find matching IDs from both data sources. Consequently, these mismatches result in null 'associateType' values in the resulting DataFrame.
In addition to the ID generation issue, there could be a problem with the data generation for other fields required for the computation of 'associateType'. Specifically, the 'getAssociates' function in CreateCaseClass.scala derives the 'isBusiness' flag from the 'isRelationshipBusiness' field. If the generator does not correctly generate this 'isRelationshipBusiness' field, it could lead to null 'isBusiness' flags and, consequently, null 'associateType' fields.
To sum up, the root cause of this issue seems to be two-fold:
• Random generation of IDs ('personEntityId' and 'relatedPersonEntityID') leading to mismatches during join and groupBy operations.

• Incomplete or incorrect generation of fields required for the computation of 'associateType', specifically, the 'isRelationshipBusiness' field.
To rectify these issues, we need to ensure that the generator produces consistent IDs and correctly generates necessary fields. Further, the handling of the 'isBusiness' flag in CreateCaseClass.scala script needs to be validated and corrected if necessary. By addressing these two aspects, we should be able to resolve the null 'associateType' issue.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

The data generator should ensure the following:

The CreateCaseClass.scala script should ensure the following:

• Accurate assignment of 'associateType' based on the 'isBusiness' flag and matching IDs.
By the end of testing, no null 'associateType' values should be present in the resulting data when using the generated data.

tickets.summary: [TIMEBOX] Automate Data Lineage for Data Packs
tickets.description:
The objective of this task is to automate data lineage for Data Packs. The primary focus is to monitor the transformation of fields (columns) from the initial raw data to the final data document model. By doing so, we aim to enhance our comprehension of the data flow and transformations, thereby bolstering data integrity and reliability.
Our current investigation revolves around examining existing tools and methodologies for tracking data lineage. We will research techniques such as data dyeing and tools like Spline and Marquez, as well as utilizing metadata and the logical execution plan in Spark. Both Spline and Marquez are open source tools which provide functionalities for monitoring data lineage in Spark applications. Spline appears to be a more fitting solution for our needs due to its effortless integration with Spark and superior data visualization capabilities.
We are also considering the utilization of DataFrame Metadata in Spark. Column metadata, a feature of Spark Datasets, can be employed to monitor data lineage. It is used by data pipelines to denote variable types and levels, and can also be manually set. This metadata can be linked to Parquet files and reloaded later, offering a potential method for tracking data lineage. Another strategy involves leveraging Spark's Logical Execution Plan tree and incorporating this logic into the core parts of processing applications.
The Data Dyeing technique is a novel approach under consideration. It involves altering the raw data ingested in parquet files, substituting each field (except IDs and node-related fields) with a unique string that merges the data source and the field name. This process, similar to 'dyeing' the data, tags each field with its origin. The data is then rewritten back into the parquet files, replacing the original data. This method offers a high level of transparency into data lineage, as the data source can be traced by examining the unique values for each field within the Document Data Model.
However, the data dyeing technique has its drawbacks—it may not effectively trace the data lineage of parsed fields. To mitigate this, we're contemplating the qmodel function mapping approach. This method uses qmodel files to extract vital data transformation details, thereby improving the accuracy of data lineage tracking. Regex matching is employed to extract the function details and the corresponding input fields.
The final solution is yet to be determined. We are open to finding the most effective approach based on our research and evaluation. Our goal is to find a balance between automation and manual verification to ensure the most precise and dependable data lineage tracking.
tickets.creator: James Lee
tickets.acceptance_criteria: Data lineage table should be produced automatically when there is a change in the data pack io schemas, or when there’s a new data pack added

tickets.summary: Integration Tests assert Functional Guarantees
tickets.description:
The integration tests should catch breaking output changes within a functional major version.
Each new major version, we will:
• Version control a major version benchmark output from integration tests (DocumentIndexInput) as a .json
Then the integration test itself will:
• Run ETL against generated raw data (already exists)

• Compare the output document, attributes and compounds against the benchmark stored in version control

• Throw an error if output of existing fields, attributes or compounds has changed
Note that the generated data will need to be seeded if it isn’t already, so that it’s always the same
See  for example of this Integration Test

tickets.summary: Fix Data Generator in Open Corporates data pack to produce correctly joined CSVs
tickets.description:
In the Open Corporates data pack, it has been observed that the data generator is not producing CSVs that join together properly. This issue stems from the CreateCaseClass stage which is joining tables together based on both the CompanyNumber and the JuristictionCode.
The problem is that in the generated CSVs, the JuristictionCode isn’t matched with CompanyNumber. This results in missing officers in the output of CreateCaseClass made from generated data. It's important to note that this issue might be impacting the overall integrity of the data and consequently the functionality of the data pack.
A tentative fix for this issue has been proposed in this PR. The implemented solution seems to rectify the problem as officers now appear in the CreateCaseClass output. However, the proposed change appears to have broken the slowtest on Jenkins, probably due to data shape changes.
The reporter has generated the data needed for their use, but this issue may impact future users. Therefore, it is recommended that the team reviews this fix, assesses the impact on the slowtest, and explores if there's a more robust solution to be implemented.
Original report on Slack
Steps to Reproduce original issue in the main branch:
• Navigate to the Open Corporates data pack in the data-packs repo in the main branch

• Run the data generator.

• Run CreateCaseClass on generated data.

• Observe the absence of officers in the output.
Steps to Reproduce temporary fix and slowTest failure on CPT-8907 branch:
• Checkout to the CPT-8907 branch.

• Run the data generator.

• Run CreateCaseClass on generated data.

• Observe the correct officers in the output.

• Run slowtest and observe the failure.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• The CreateCaseClass stage should correctly join tables based on both the CompanyNumber and JuristictionCode.

• The output of CreateCaseClass should show the correct officers without any inconsistencies.

• All other generated CSVs should join properly, maintaining data integrity.

• The slowtest on Jenkins should pass without any failure, ensuring that the changes have not introduced new issues.

tickets.summary: Generators should accept a seed for reproducible output
tickets.description:
Context
The data-packs repo contains raw data generators which use a library to generate random values for the raw document fields. Some of these already accept seeds though they don’t seem to be fully implemented.
Steps
• data-pack-bvd generator should be deterministic as it uses a seed, but it isn’t. We want to identify why the fields are generating different values in runs using the same seed. Find an example of a field that’s non-deterministic, and identify when the randomness is being added and how to fix this.
• Run the generator twice and compare the csv output. Understand which fields are non-deterministic.

• Narrow down which function call is non-deterministic and why.

• Add seed argument to calls to the random library, look at project-example, demo-banking or data-pack-bvd for how to approach doing this. One thing to note is that the seed should be modified deterministically with multiplication/addition within docs, so that e.g. a director gets a different random value to the root business.
tickets.creator: James Lee

tickets.summary: Codebase Cleanup: Refactoring and Optimizing Project Components
tickets.description:
This story aims to improve the overall quality and maintainability of the trials-platform repo by focusing on codebase cleanup and refactoring. We will systematically address specific areas of the code, including the UI, to enhance readability, performance, and maintainability.
The cleanup process will involve:
• Removing unused imports and dependencies, which can lead to reduced bundle size and faster build times.

• Eliminating commented out code, which can clutter the codebase and make it harder for other developers to understand the intent of the code.

• Simplifying and streamlining logic, such as complex nested if statements, switch cases, and loops, to improve readability and reduce the likelihood of bugs.

• Identifying and refactoring any duplicated code, to adhere to the DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle.

• Ensuring consistent coding style and formatting throughout the codebase, to make it easier for developers to navigate and understand the project structure.

• Reviewing and updating documentation, including comments, READMEs, and code comments, to reflect the current state of the project accurately.

• Where relevant and applicable, analyzing and optimizing database queries, API calls, and other performance-related aspects to enhance the overall efficiency and response times.
In addition to the main ticket, we will create sub-tickets for specific parts of the cleanup, such as the UI, to ensure a thorough and systematic approach to the cleanup process. These sub-tickets will allow team members to focus on designated areas and coordinate their efforts effectively.
By implementing this codebase cleanup, we aim to improve the overall health of the trials-platform repo and ensure a more efficient and pleasant development experience for the team.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani

tickets.summary: Implement Script for Efficient Remote Desktop Access to GCP VMs
tickets.description:
Connecting directly to a Google Cloud Platform (GCP) VM or VDI remote desktop using a VNC viewer with an SSH tunnel and a secure Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) tunnel offers several advantages over using Apache Guacamole:
• Reduced latency: When using Apache Guacamole, the remote desktop data has to go through the Guacamole server, which can add some latency to the connection. On the other hand, when connecting directly via a VNC viewer and an SSH tunnel with IAP, the connection is established directly between the local machine and the GCP remote desktop, which can potentially result in lower latency.

• Better performance: VNC viewer applications can be optimized for performance, while Apache Guacamole relies on the performance of the user's web browser. A VNC viewer can leverage local resources and hardware acceleration to provide a smoother experience, whereas a web-based solution may not be as efficient.

• More control over connection settings: With a VNC viewer, you can fine-tune connection settings, such as color depth and compression, to suit your requirements and network conditions. This level of customization may not be available or as flexible when using Apache Guacamole.

• Improved security: Set up a secure connection using an SSH tunnel, which provides an encrypted connection between your local machine and the GCP remote desktop. Additionally, using a secure IAP tunnel adds an extra layer of security by enabling context-aware access controls and securing the connection to the remote desktop. While Apache Guacamole supports SSL/TLS encryption, the direct VNC connection over SSH with IAP can offer enhanced security by limiting access to the remote desktop through the combined tunnels.

• Reach out to cloud setting out advantages

• Reviewing Usman’s implementation if we get the go-ahead
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria:
• Check the GCP VM/VDI status and offer to start it: The script should verify if the target GCP VM is running and, if not, ask the user if they want to start it. If the user agrees, the script should initiate the VM and proceed to establish the VNC connection.

• Determine the VNC port: The script should automatically detect the VNC port on the GCP VM or allow the user to specify the remote port. 

• Create a secure tunnel: The script should create an SSH tunnel combined with an IAP tunnel.

• Retrieve and encrypt the VNC password: The script should obtain the VNC password from the VM and encrypt it using the DES algorithm with the provided key and initialization vector.

• Establish a VNC connection: The script should start the VNC viewer with the obtained VNC password and port number, allowing the user to access the remote VM via VNC.
tickets.testing_required: Check that the user can establish a remote desktop connection to the green VDI. To make this work, the user will need to install a VNC viewer, which can be done via the Heimdal Agent.

tickets.summary: Enable IAP for Trials-Platform repo
tickets.description:
On a gradle build, there's a task called installNPM which executes a node script which populates the _authToken property in ~/.npmrc which is then used to make requests over IAP. This token is determined by the stuff we set in the gradle.properties, such as the client secret or refresh token.
However when we do a gradle build with the trials-platform project, this doesn't happen automatically, so we need to add this in by default rather than manually running npm run refresh-iap-token.
tickets.creator: Usman Kayani
tickets.acceptance_criteria: The trials-platform repo can be compiled over IAP and VPN

tickets.summary: SaaS access: solar-1-saas-disc-1
tickets.description:
Checklist Item Name
Requirement
Notes
Required for VDI-only users?
Complete?
Solar GCP Auth Group Membership
[Solar only]
Ensure membership of the auth.gcp@quantexa.com Azure group
This is needed for anyone who will access a Solar VDI.
If not already a member of this group raise an IO ticket to get added.
You can validate whether you are a member of this group using the Azure Portal - search for ‘Auth GCP Users’ and view the Members list.
Yes
Completed
Orientation Pre-Requisites
Complete the Pre-requisites section on Orientation Materials
These are some key steps that will allow a smooth onboarding process
Yes
Completed
Read Orientation Materials
Go through the content on the Orientation Materials page.
This is required so that you are familiar with the processes surrounding the Cloud Platform.
You’re not expected to follow each guide, but get an awareness of what content exists.
Mandatory pages in Overview and VDI sections only.
Completed
Read Cloud Platform Support Process
Cloud Platform Support Process
Confirm you have read and understood how to raise requests for help of different types.
Yes
Completed
Atlassian cloud-platform-users Group Membership
Get added to Atlassian group for access to this site and CPT board - cloud-platform-users. This can be achieved with a User access request.
 
Yes
Completed
QCP Slack Channel Membership
Get on slack channels: #qcp-support, #qcp-users, #qcp-announcements
Ask an existing member to add you (eg team mate)
Yes
Completed
Solar Security Training
Complete KnowBe4 training course Cloud Technical End User Development Standard
This requirement may be waived if you will not be accessing or deploying code to a Solar environment.
Users can add themselves to the team called “KB4-QCP Solar Users” in Microsoft Teams using the join code lq5fk2a , alternatively project managers can join the team (they also need to complete the training so they understand the requirements on their project) and add the project team members. Once the user is a member of this team, within 24 hours they should receive a notification of the assigned training.
‘Keeping Sensitive / Highly Confidential Data within Solar’ only
Completed
Post Onboarding Checklist Ticket
Jira created containing post onboarding checklist in project JIRA.
After a user has been onboarded, there are a few more actions to take. You must create a ticket to ensure these are not forgotten.
A ticket must be created within the user's own Jira project (eg a team member is using the OFX project team a ticket on the OFX board).
This ticket should contain the post-onboarding checklist which is available further down this page.
Be sure to:
• Assign the ticket to the relevant individual

• Schedule the ticket into sprint/kanban

• Using a proper Jira ticket link with “caused by” to link it to the IO ticket.

• Copy the below post onboarding checklist into the Jira ticket to allow tracking to completion.
Environment Quick Start Guide
Overview and step 1 only.
Completed

tickets.summary: Move data pack utils into etl-libraries
tickets.description:
Context: We’re simplifying the mono repo code base by moving functionality that is (or should be) shared across all data packs into a common module called “etl-libraries”.
• Create a new submodule in etl-libraries, probably called “etl-utils”

• Review util functions in each data pack, they’re generally called “ProjectETLUtils” or similar - if they generalise, ie they’re not using business logic specific to that data pack, they should be moved to the new submodule
• Move the instance of the function to a shared location, updating all references to it with the new location

• Check if similar or identical function exists in any other data packs. If so, change the reference to it and carefully compare logic to ensure no functional changes occur

• Add docstrings to the functions and review their readability - this is our chance to improve their quality

• Regression test the mono-repo ETLs
tickets.creator: James Lee

subtasks.summary: Regression test on generated data
Refactor utils into etl-libraries

tickets.summary: Add Trafficking Data Source
tickets.description:
Background
Add the deprecated datapacks to the trials-platform, Laundromat, Trafficking and WealthX. These are no longer required to be maintained as fully-supported datapacks but are still required by in the trials-platform demo.
Add the data source using the repo-tool (etl prompt) then copy across the relevant ETL code from the datapack. Ensure the UI works successfully with each of these data sources configures.
Helpful Links
• trial-platform:  

• ondemand:  

• trafficking:  

• repo tool prompt for adding feature: https://docs.quantexa.com/reference-component/2.3.0/reference/testing-tools-resources/repository-tool/adding.html#optional-services
Summary of Steps
• Use repo tool to add data source https://docs.quantexa.com/reference-component/2.3.0/reference/data-integration/data-fusion/generator/adding-new-data-source.html

• Copy across each data source’s ETL code from data pack repo. Each scala module in data-pack-wealthx goes to a module in trials-platform:
• data-source-trafficking→ data-source-trafficking

• etl-trafficking-data-generator → trials-platform-data-generator

• etl-trafficking-etl-common → data-source-common:common-etl

• etl-trafficking-models-common → data-source-common:common-model
At this point, add if not already traffickingto the datapackModels, datapackEtl, datapackExtractors, datapacksGenerators defined in libraries.gradle. We need to make sure running .\gradlew data-source-all:projectShadowJar data-source-all:dependencyShadowJar contains trafficking. Then we can add traffickingto the etl.json so that the runEtl scripts will run ETL for trafficking as well.

• The resolver config and UI code should already be there

• Run the apps
tickets.creator: Daniela Casilli
